# Record of Revisions
The LASC General Rules (LGR) document was created to consolidate team organization requirements into a single reference. As a result, the rules and requirements related to team and mission organization are now contained in this document.
The table below records all revisions of this document since the first Latin American Space Challenge.
| Edition Number | Revision Number | Issue Date | Effective Date |
|------------------|-------------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | 1 | March 2026 | March 2026 |
<!-- image -->

# LGR First Edition
The following table describes changes contained in Edition 1 of the LASC General Rules (LGR). The table describes significant changes and individual changes in each of the LGR sections and provisions.
| LGR 1 Revision 1 Highlights | LGR 1 Revision 1 Highlights |
|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Area(s) of Change | Description(s) |
| All Document. | New document. |
<!-- image -->

# Purpose
The Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) General Rules (LGR) establish the rules, requirements, and guidelines necessary for teams to effectively participate in the event's challenges.
This document serves as the primary reference for teams throughout the Application and Registration Process and during event participation, defining the criteria, procedures, and practices to be followed.
The LGR provides the overarching framework for team organization and mission development, enabling participants to align with established best practices in safety, quality, and mission planning.

# Manual Revisions
The document is subject to an annual revision, which invariably leads to a new edition. In the event that critical issues emerge impacting the content of the document, a revision to the current edition will be undertaken. All changes in this document are listed in the revision highlights table. For easier orientation, the following symbols identify any changes made within each section:
- Addition of a new item.
- Change to an item.
- Deletion of an item.

# Section 1 - Safety (SAF)
Section 1 details the Safety (SAF) policies, procedures, and processes required for all teams, missions, and members to ensure event safety. This section states our Safety Policy.

# 1.1. Safety Policy
Safety is our Value number 1.
The Safety Management System (SMS) of the Latin American Space Challenge is composed of four pillars: Safety Policy, Safety Risk Management, Safety Assurance, and Safety Promotion.
All team members shall report any observed unsafe activities to event officials. The event will provide a specific channel for these reports, which will be anonymized before any potential action is taken by event officials.
The event will be guided by a Just Culture approach, where errors will be addressed through reeducation. However, any violation of rules will be treated as potential unruly behavior, with penalties or disqualification possible as outlined in Section 2.
During the Launch Readiness Review (LRR), event officials will use a checklist to approve an experimental rocket for loading with energetics and a rocket motor. Ensuring safety will be a key process for issuing any Flight Card during the event.
Event officials will conduct a safety risk management analysis before each launch to reduce the potential probabilities and/or severities of any operational risks.
Promotion is key to raising the bar among all participating teams. Event officials will conduct safety briefings to increase overall public awareness. Launching an experimental rocket is not trivial; the event has associated risks. All participants shall understand and accept these risks by signing a Waiver and Release of Liability to participate.

ORG 1.1.1. All participants shall follow the Latin American Space Challenge Safety Policy.
<!-- image -->

# Section 2 - Documentation System (DOC)
Section 2 details the LASC Documentation System (DOC) policies, procedures, and processes required for all teams, missions, and members to ensure the proper organization of the entry application, team and mission management, and compliance with the general rules of the event.

# 2.1. Official Languages
English, Portuguese and Spanish are the official languages of the Latin American Space Challenge; documents comprising the LASC Documentation System are written in International English in accordance with the official policy.
ORG 2.1.1. All documents, including reports, presentation materials, and correspondence with event officials shall be in English. For oral presentations, including videos and on-site presentations, speakers have the flexibility to choose from any of the event's official languages.

# 2.2. Documentation System
The LASC Documentation System is structured into three hierarchical levels of documents that establish the overall framework of the event and its challenges. Adherence to this documentation system by teams and participants is mandatory to ensure safety and compliance. In the event of any conflict or inconsistency, higher-level documents shall prevail over lower-level documents.
The system comprises: (1) Policies and Rules; (2) Standard Manuals and Procedures; and (3) Announcements, Forms, and Templates.
Figure 1: LASC Documentation System.
<!-- image -->
All documents within the LASC Documentation System are made publicly available in a timely manner. Documents and forms referenced in this manual can be accessed and downloaded from the LASC website (www.lasc.space).
<!-- image -->
Depending on the objective and purpose of each document, LASC Standards and Recommended Practices (LSARPs) are provided to support participants in identifying mandatory and recommended requirements.
ORG 2.2.1. All participants shall regularly access the LASC website (www.lasc.space) to review the most current versions of all applicable documents. Downloading documents for local use is discouraged, as revisions may occur periodically. The use of outdated versions of LASC documentation is not permitted and may result in penalties.

# 2.3. LASC Standards and Recommended Practices (LSARPs)
The specifications and standards outlined in reputable aerospace guidelines and industry best practices form the primary sources for the LASC Standards and Recommended Practices (LSARPs). These encompass a wide range of criteria, extending to various aspects of rocket design, engineering, and operational management.
The LSARPs draw upon established principles from recognized aerospace entities, technical publications, and regulatory frameworks relevant to space engineering and mission planning.
The sources of reference and benchmarking includes, but are not limited to the Intercollegiate Rocket Engineering Competition (IREC) or Spaceport America Cup (SA Cup), the European Rocketry Challenge (EuRoC), Friends of Amateur Rocketry - Oxidizers Uninhibited Tournament (FAR-OUT), the Launch Canada Challenge, the IATA Operational Safety Audit (IOSA) program, the Manual de Segurança e Boas Práticas para Operação e Lançamento de Foguetes Amadores by the Brazilian Space Agency (AEB), and the NASA System Safety Handbook.

# Explanation of LSARPs
LSARPs in this manual are designed for use within the Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) and provide the criteria for evaluations. LSARPs are not regulations.

# LSARPs Identifiers
All provisions (i. e. the LSARPs) are marked with an identifier consisting of a three-letter section abbreviation and a series of three numbers separated by two decimal points (e.g., ORG 1.1.1).
Maintaining stable LSARPs identifiers is crucial for user convenience among teams, mission, judges, and others, and for maintaining accurate statistical data. Therefore, efforts are made to minimize renumbering of LSARPs when revising the documentation.

# Standards
LASC Standards are specified systems, policies, programs, processes, procedures, plans, sets of measures, components, types of equipment or any other aspect under the scope of LASC that have been determined to be a necessity, and with which a team will be expected to be in conformity during the evaluation process.
<!-- image -->
Standards always contain the words ' shall ' or ' must ' (e.g., 'The team shall have a process…' or 'Each mission must include…') to indicate that conformance by a team and/or mission under evaluation is mandatory for all space challenges.

# Recommended Practices
LASC Recommended Practices are specified systems, policies, programs, processes, procedures, plans, sets of measures, components, types of equipment or any other aspects under the scope of LASC that have been determined to be desirable, but conformance is optional by a team and/or a mission.
Recommended Practices always contain the word ' should ' (e.g., 'The team should have a policy…') to denote conformance is optional.

# Conditional Phrase
Certain provisions (i.e., standards or recommended practices, or sub-specifications within certain provisions), begin with a conditional phrase. The conditional phrase states the conditions (one or more) that serve to define the applicability of the provision or sub-specification to the team and/or mission being evaluated. A conditional phrase begins with the words 'If the team…'
When assessing a team against a provision or sub-specification that begins with a conditional phrase, the Judge will first determine if a team meets the condition(s) stated in the conditional phrase. If the team and/or mission meets the stated condition(s), the provision is applicable to the team and shall be assessed for conformance. If the team does not meet the condition(s), the provision or specification is not applicable, and such non-applicability will then be recorded as N/A.
<!-- image -->

# Section 3 - Team Organization (ORG)
Section 2 defines the Team Organization (ORG) addressing the organization and management system of a team for the purpose of ensuring conformity with all provisions of the event.

# 3.1. Team Composition and Eligibility
The Latin American Space Challenge allows teams to submit multiple missions across different challenges and categories. The organization of each team and its assigned missions forms the foundation for the successful execution of every selected mission. The following sections outline the minimum required composition, organizational structure, and eligibility criteria.

# Team Composition
ORG 3.1.1. Teams may be composed of:
- Student members: Individuals currently enrolled in high school, technical school, a bachelor's, or a master's degree program, as well as those who were officially enrolled during the previous academic year (e.g., recent graduates who completed their studies shortly before the event are still eligible);
- Non-student members: Individuals such as enthusiasts, researchers, startup members, amateurs, and hobbyists; or
- Mixed: A combination of student and non-student members.
ORG 3.1.2. There is no maximum limit on the total number of members per team. However, each team shall have a minimum of four (4) enrolled members, corresponding to the minimum structure required for a mission. Each individual shall participate in only one team.
ORG 3.1.3. Each team must designate a Team Leader during the Application and Registration Process. Additionally, each mission must have a designated Mission Leader. The same individual may serve as both the Team Leader and the Mission Leader.
ORG 3.1.4. Each team shall designate a Responsible Person who shall be legally accountable for the team and all its members, including any actions, omissions, or incidents arising from the team's activities. The Responsible Person shall be identified in the Institutional Participation Letter described in Section 2.4 and may serve as its signatory.

This individual shall be liable, in accordance with Brazilian legislation, including but not limited to the Código Civil Brasileiro (Lei nº 10.406/2002) and the Código Penal Brasileiro (Decreto-Lei nº 2.848/1940), for any damages to persons, property, or the environment, as well as for any injuries or incidents resulting from negligence, imprudence, or malpractice.
The Responsible Person must be at least 18 (eighteen) years old and must formally acknowledge their role by signing a Term of Responsibility to be submitted timely before the on-site event.
<!-- image -->
Figure 2: Example of a Minimum Team Organizational Structure.
<!-- image -->
ORG 3.1.5. Teams composed of individuals under eighteen (18) years of age shall submit a signed authorization from their parents and/or legal guardians on or before the Mission Report submission deadline. Teams with minors who are not legally authorized to participate shall be excluded from the event.

# Mission Structure
The Latin American Space Challenge comprises three challenges, each with distinct categories. Teams shall submit a separate Entry Application Form for each mission in order to be considered in the Application and Registration Process as described in Section 3. The relationship between each team and the event organization is established through its selected missions.
ORG 3.1.6. Each mission within a team shall establish an organizational structure that includes, at a minimum, the following positions: Mission Leader, Safety Manager, Operations Manager, and Technology Manager. Descriptions and responsibilities are provided in Appendix A.
Figure 3: Minimum Mission Organizational Structure Required.
<!-- image -->
<!-- image -->
ORG 3.1.7. Each required position within a mission shall be assigned to a distinct individual to ensure clear distribution of responsibilities and accountability. An individual may hold required positions across multiple missions; however, no individual shall hold more than one required position within the same mission
ORG 3.1.8. For missions submitted to the Rocket Challenge, the assignment of a Rocketry Mentor (RM) may be required. The conditions under which a Rocketry Mentor is mandatory are defined in Section 3.2.

# 3.2. Rocketry Mentor (RM)
Teams with selected missions in the Rocket Challenge and/or Lander Challenge are encouraged to have an experienced Rocketry Mentor (RM) assisting the mission development and, mainly, advising on safety procedures. The RM is not required to be onsite during the launch activities.
ORG 3.2.1. Each team should designate a Rocketry Mentor for each Selected Mission. This is particularly mandatory for new teams that have not previously participated in the Latin American Space Challenge or for teams selected to participate in a mission category that involves hybrid or liquid rocket propulsion systems.
The RM will follow the team's progress throughout its developments and make recommendations and/or modifications on the project in order to guarantee safety and increase chances of a successful launch.
ORG 3.2.2. Each selected mission should register a RM by the deadline defined in the LASC Event Guidance and Milestones (EGM) document. Event officials will contact the RM to confirm their information and verify their acceptance to serve as the mission's mentor.
ORG 3.2.3. The same RM may be designated by different teams for up to three (3) selected missions.
ORG 3.2.4. The RM shall be an engineer with proven experience in the space or defense sector, or an engineer who has previously participated in the Latin American Space Challenge.

ORG 3.2.3. The same RM may be designated by different teams for up to three (3) selected missions.
ORG 3.2.4. The RM shall be an engineer with proven experience in the space or defense sector, or an engineer who has previously participated in the Latin American Space Challenge.
This requirement ensures that the mentor has the necessary expertise and a solid understanding of the mission's dynamics and associated risks. LASC reserves the right to reject the proposed Rocketry Mentor at its sole discretion and without the need for justification.

# 3.3. Foreign Teams and Seeking a Visa
For many foreign teams we have seen before, it is important to seek a visa upon acceptance. Keep in mind that some members of your team may not be citizens of the country where your university is located. Therefore, upon acceptance into the challenge, file for your visas immediately.
<!-- image -->
If an individual on your team is from a country that has difficulties acquiring a visa, be sure to cross-train replacements in case they are unable to attend the challenge. Ensure that all necessary paperwork is submitted promptly so that team members can attend the competition in person, rather than as a photo affixed to their control panel.
ORG 3.3.1. Each foreign team with at least one selected mission shall contact event officials on or before submitting Progress Update 1 to request a Letter of Invitation. The mission leader shall provide a list of full names, passport numbers, email addresses, and mobile numbers of all team members seeking a visa to Brazil.

# 3.4. Institutional Participation Letter
The Latin American Space Challenge requires that every participating team, regardless of the number of missions or challenges they enter, shall show an affiliation with an academic institution, a company, or an accountable individual who is over the Brazilian legal age of 18. This accountable party shall sign a letter accepting full responsibility for the team members, projects and all activities related to their participation in the event.
Institutions sending more than one team to the LASC need only to write one participation letter, covering all their teams, but each included team shall submit an individual copy of that letter.
ORG 3.4.1. All teams shall timely send a digital PDF copy of the letter signed by a representative of the institution or responsible individual, acknowledging full responsibility for the team. The signatory shall be a senior faculty member (i.e., professor) or a senior representative of the team (i.e., aerospace/aeronautical or mechanical engineer).

# 3.5. Participant Agreement
All participants shall sign the LASC Participant Agreement (Waiver and Release of Liability), without exception.
To the extent permitted by applicable law, each team shall indemnify and hold harmless LASC and its event officials from and against any and all claims, demands, actions, liabilities, damages, losses, or expenses (including, but not limited to, monetary loss, property damage, personal injury, or wrongful death), whether asserted by an individual or entity, or arising from judicial or administrative proceedings at any governmental level, resulting from any acts, omissions, negligence, or willful misconduct by the team, its affiliated institution, or their respective officers, members, employees, agents, contractors, invitees, or volunteers. This includes, without limitation, the payment of any penalties, fines, awards, fees, and related costs or expenses.
ORG 3.5.1. All individuals attending the event, including team members, faculty advisors, and any other participants, shall sign the LASC Participant Agreement. Individuals who fail to sign the agreement shall not be permitted to participate in any activities at the Sugarcane Launch Range (SLR) or access event facilities.
<!-- image -->
ORG 3.5.2. The LASC Participant Agreement will be sent by e-mail to all officially registered participants after the payment of the Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket, but before the submission of the Mission Report, and shall be digitally signed.

<!-- image -->
ORG 3.5.2. The LASC Participant Agreement will be sent by e-mail to all officially registered participants after the payment of the Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket, but before the submission of the Mission Report, and shall be digitally signed.
ORG 3.5.3. If a registered participant fails to sign the LASC Participant Agreement online before the deadline, all missions associated with that team member will be subject to penalties. Although penalties may apply, there will be a final opportunity to sign the agreement during the check-in session.
Participants attending competition who do not sign this waiver will not be permitted to enter the event facilities.

# 3.6. Insurance
The organization of the event will not be responsible or pay for any accidents, damaged property, and injuries related to the event and caused by selected mission; including if a team's activity damages a person or property. Also, if the person or property owner decides to sue the team, the event's policy does not protect the team from the additional lawsuit.
ORG 3.6.1. All teams should obtain and provide a proof of insurance coverage for all attending members prior to attending the Latin American Space Challenge. Note: individual, personal, or travel insurance policies do not qualify under this position. LASC is not responsible for and cannot assist in finding suitable insurance policies.
# 3.7. Unruly Behavior, Disqualification, Withdrawal

# Penalties for Unsafe or Unsportsmanlike Conduct
Unsafe conduct includes, but is not limited to, violating any of the established principles stated on LASC Documents, failure to use checklists during operations, violating motor vehicle traffic safety rules, and failure to use appropriate personal protective equipment.
ORG 3.7.1. Team Conduct It is the responsibility of every Mission Leader and Safety Manager to ensure that their team conducts themselves in a safe and professional manner. There is a list of point deductions per occurrence that is set prior to the competition for various types of infraction, ranging from not clearing an area in time when a salvo is announced to providing false information on a report.
Unsportsmanlike conduct also includes, but is not limited to, hostility shown towards any LASC participant and officials, intentional misrepresentation of facts to any competition official, intentional failure to comply with any reasonable instruction given by an event official.
ORG 3.7.2. Teams will be penalized for every instance of unsafe or unsportsmanlike conduct recorded by event officials depending on the severity of the incident.
<!-- image -->

# Disqualification
A number of criteria constitute grounds for disqualification from consideration for any award and continuation at the event. These can include a failure to meet the defining mission requirements, failure to submit any document and failure to send eligible team members to the onsite event.
Substance abuse and intoxication (or after-effects thereof) during the event and purposeful endangering behaviors severely compromising the safety of LASC and respective participants will make the entire team immediately and without further warning, eligible for expulsion from the LASC event in disgrace. If one or more members of a team fails to be utterly sober and clear-headed, this is regarded as outright contempt of the LASC spirit and safety guidelines. The consequence is the immediate and irrevocable removal of the team from the LASC event.
ORG 3.7.3. Event officials reserve the right to assess any misconduct/mismanagement case by case and to take the necessary proper actions leading to penalties or disqualification of specific team members, mission or the the entire team.

# 3.8. Mission Withdrawal
ORG 3.8.1. Teams that decide to formally withdraw a mission from the Space Challenge at any time prior to the event shall send an e-mail entitled 'Mission Your Mission ID Formally Withdraws From The Event Edition LASC'' to lasc@lasc.space.
For example, a team with a mission assigned the Mission ID "19" would withdraw from the 7th LASC by sending an e-mail entitled 'Mission 19 Formally Withdraws from the 7th LASC''.
<!-- image -->

# Section 4 - Application and Registration Process (REG)
Section 4 defines the Application and Registration Process (REG), including the procedures, requirements, and timelines for the submission of mission proposals, as well as the evaluation, selection, and registration of participating teams and their respective missions.

# 4.1. Entry Application Process
ORG 4.1.1. Teams intending to participate in any challenge shall submit a complete Entry Application Form for each mission category in which they intend to participate, using the form available on the LASC website during the period defined by the event officials. Late submissions shall not be accepted.

# Submission Limitations
ORG 41.2. Each team may submit multiple missions across different challenges and categories. However, no more than three (3) missions per team shall be selected in total by the event officials.
ORG 4.1.3. A team shall not have more than one selected mission within the same category of a given challenge.
As a result, no team may compete in the same category more than once. The event officials will monitor and assess each project independently, regardless of shared student membership or academic affiliation. For example, Team ABC may register a project for the 1 km AGL apogee with hybrid/liquid propulsion system category and another experimental rocket project for the 0.5 km AGL apogee project.

# Entry Application Form and Announcement of Selected Missions
The Entry Application Form is made available on the LASC website during the application period, as defined in the LASC Event Guidance and Milestones (EGM) document. No payment is required at the time of submission.
ORG 4.1.4. Total completeness of the Entry Form is required, including information specified on Section 3 of this document.
Selected Missions (Tier 1 and Tier 2) will be announced on the LASC website following the application deadline, through the publication of an official Mission ID list.

# Mission Identification Number
All selected missions shall be assigned a unique Mission Identification Number (Mission ID) for identification and tracking purposes. Mission IDs are assigned independently of team
<!-- image -->
identification, and no direct association between teams and Mission ID numbering shall be inferred.
Missions participating in the Rocket Challenge shall be assigned Mission IDs ranging from 001 to 199. Missions participating in the Satellite Challenge shall be assigned Mission IDs ranging from 201 to 399, and missions participating in the Lander Challenge shall be assigned Mission IDs ranging from 401 to 600.
The Mission ID serves as the primary reference for all official identification and tracking of missions by the event organization.
ORG 4.1.5. A correspondence between a team with a selected mission and event officials shall contain the respective Mission's ID number to enable a more timely and accurate response.

# 4.2. Mission Selection Process
While the event officials aim to admit all applicants, a formal selection process is required to ensure the safe and effective management of team participation. The selection process is not conducted on a first-come, first-served basis; all applications submitted within the designated application period are evaluated on equal terms.
The Application and Registration Process is governed by the LASC Mission Selection Process, which is based on the availability of operational slots.
Slots are limited and may be affected by external constraints, including, but not limited to, capacity limits within each challenge, infrastructure constraints, and adverse weather conditions. Accordingly, event officials select a predetermined number of missions based on criteria established to ensure safety and maximize the overall quality of the event.
Selection criteria include, but are not limited to, compliance with applicable documentation, the quality and objectives of the proposed mission, the level of technological development and innovation, and the team's experience and demonstrated maturity in past events.
Given the historically high volume of applications, teams should be prepared for the possibility that their missions may not be selected. Due to the limited number of available slots, selected missions are classified as Tier 1 or Tier 2.

Given the historically high volume of applications, teams should be prepared for the possibility that their missions may not be selected. Due to the limited number of available slots, selected missions are classified as Tier 1 or Tier 2.
- Tier 1 Selected Missions will be assigned and reserved a launch slot during the onsite event in compliance with the rules outlined in Section 3 of this document.
- Tier 2 Selected Missions will not have guaranteed slots during the onsite event. However, they may be allocated vacant or newly available slots during operations.
In addition, throughout the delivery roadmap, if a Tier 1 Selected Mission withdraws, an appropriate Level 2 Selected Mission will be escalated as a Tier 1 Selected Mission.
<!-- image -->
Figure 1: Mission Selection Process.
<!-- image -->
Regardless of the outcome, all teams will be notified about the results of the selection process, including feedback on their submissions up to 90 (ninety) days after the closing of the applications.
It is important to note that submissions received after the application deadline will not be considered under any circumstances.
<!-- image -->

# Section 5 - Awards (AWA)
Section 5 defines the Overall Awards and Challenge Awards of the Latin American Space Challenge, including the general rules for scoring and evaluation, the scoreboard structure, and the high-level criteria for the allocation of Overall Awards.

# 5.1. Scores and Scoreboard
Scores and grading criteria are defined in the respective challenge standard manuals, according to the objectives, purpose, and specificities of each challenge.

# Scoreboard
After each appropriate milestone, event officials will publish a partial scoreboard for all mission categories. The partial scoreboard will be available on the LASC's MissionForge Platform and will be updated after the evaluation of each selected mission. The MissionForge Platform will be available for participants of selected missions after the first progress update.
Event officials will document the evaluations using individual score sheets for each selected mission and then consolidate them into one master scoreboard using the grading criteria set out in the following subsections.
Up to seven days after the Award Ceremony, individual scoring sheets and feedback regarding the strengths and weaknesses of each selected mission's performance will be provided by email if requested.

# Handling of Questions & Complaints Regarding Scoring
Teams are welcome to approach the officials to ask for specific, non-binding, oral feedback regarding their perception of the teams' work during all points of the competition to provide the teams with an opportunity to learn and improve.
In the case the teams have more detailed questions or specific complaints regarding the scoring after the scoring has been announced, such as they would like to receive elaborate feedback on a particular aspect of the score for clarification, e.g., to improve upon for the next event, or if they identify an honest mistake made by the jury, the following process applies:
ORG 5.1.2. Only the mission leader can submit a written feedback request once to lasc@lasc.space. Submissions of the feedback are accepted until no later than one week (7 days) after official announcement of the score. To keep the workload on the officials to a reasonable amount, teams are asked to limit their questions plus complaints to 3 (three) in total. Event officials will then review these three questions and/or complaints and provide written feedback.
<!-- image -->
If an honest mistake in scoring is apparent, event officials will review the score provided to the team and decide on a case-by-case basis if and how to account for this, especially and only if this would significantly affect the overall score and placement of the team.

<!-- image -->
If an honest mistake in scoring is apparent, event officials will review the score provided to the team and decide on a case-by-case basis if and how to account for this, especially and only if this would significantly affect the overall score and placement of the team.
It should be noted that teams are expected not to abuse this possibility of questions and complaints. Officers will not participate in a discussion questioning the evaluation's reasoning on the score given.

# 5.2. Overall Awards
The Latin American Space Challenge grants both Overall Awards and Challenge Awards. Overall Awards recognize achievements across the event as a whole, while Challenge Awards are specific to each challenge: Rocket Challenge, Satellite Challenge, and Lander Challenge. Awards are presented during the Award Ceremony.
The Overall Awards include: the João B. G. Canalle Award for Technical Excellence; the Rick Maschek Engineering Award for Innovation; the Valentina V. Tereshkova Award for Women Representation and Diversity; the Team Conduct Award; the Team Sportsmanship Award; and the Team Spirit Award.

# Award Ceremony
ORG 5.2.1. The Award Ceremony, to be held on the last day of the event, will be the final milestone of LASC where winners will be announced. All teams shall be present during the Awards Ceremony. If an award-winning team is absent, event officials have the right to select the subsequent team to receive the award. This ensures that recognition is given and accepted in person at the ceremony.

# João B. G. Canalle Award for Technical Excellence
The João B. G. Canalle Award for Technical Excellence is a Technical Achievement Award that recognizes a team which demonstrates exceptional overall engineering discipline and technical skill through their analyses and conclusions, project or program planning and execution, operational procedure, manufacturing processes, iterative improvement, systems engineering methodology, robust design, etc. Any team with a selected mission is eligible for the João B. G. Canalle Award for Technical Excellence, although it should have submitted Presentation Session Materials and a high-quality Mission Report.

# Rick Maschek Engineering Award for Innovation
The Rick Maschek Engineering Award for Innovation is a is a Technical Achievement Award that recognizes a team whose selected mission includes one or more features (including analytic or operational processes as well as components or assemblies) the judging panel finds genuinely "novel", "inventive", or solving a unique problem identified by the team.
<!-- image -->
Any team with a selected mission is eligible for the Rick Maschek Engineering Award for Innovation, although it should have submitted Presentation Session Materials and a high-quality Mission Report.

# Valentina V. Tereshkova Award for Women Representation and Diversity
The Valentina V. Tereshkova Award for Women Representation and Diversity recognizes a team whose members composition has a substantial percentage of women and diversity. It also recognizes teams who have women and diversity in prominent positions, such as mission leaders. Any team with a selected mission is eligible for the Valentina V. Tereshkova Award for Women Representation and Diversity, although it shall have submitted a high-quality Mission Report.

# Team Conduct Award
The Team Conduct Award recognizes a team whose conduct throughout the Latin American Space Challenge is exemplary of goals and ideals held by the event organizers. The Latin American Space Challenge should be an event where academia, industry, and the public may come together to preserve, popularize, and advance space science in a collaborative environment energized by friendly competition. The Team Conduct Award will be awarded to a single team chosen by the event officials participating in any challenge.

# Team Sportsmanship Award
The Team Sportsmanship Award recognizes a team which goes above and beyond to assist their fellow teams and the organizers assure the event is a safe, productive, and enjoyable experience for all involved. They may do this in many ways, such as making themselves available to lend-a-hand whenever and however they can (whether they are asked to or not), being positive role models for their fellow teams, and generally being a "force for good" in every activity in which they involve themselves. The Team Sportsmanship Award will be awarded to a single team chosen by the event officials participating in any challenge..

# Team Spirit Award
The Team Spirit Award recognizes a team that has displayed an outstanding effort as working as a unit towards a common goal, by being exceptionally organized, reliable, and prepared in all aspects of the competition, be it deliverables, communication, or operation, and goes above and beyond to display a great sense of team spirit and sportsmanship. The Team Spirit Award will be awarded to a single team chosen by the event officials participating in any challenge.

# 5.2. Challenge Awards
The Latin American Space Challenge awards First Place and Second Place in each Mission Category within each Challenge, as well as Overall Winners for each Challenge.
<!-- image -->

# Appendix A: Role Description
This appendix provides detailed role descriptions to support the effective organization of teams and their mission structures. It aims to guide task delegation, planning, and team coordination, helping ensure successful participation in the Latin American Space Challenge.

# Team Leader
- Serve as the primary point of contact between the team and the LASC Organization for all administrative and non-technical matters.
- Oversee the entire application and registration process for the team.
- Ensure accurate and timely submission of all required team documentation.
- Manage team logistics, including coordination of travel, lodging, and on-site support.
- Handle communications related to event fees, deadlines, and administrative compliance.
- Coordinate with Mission Leaders to ensure alignment and readiness across all missions within the team.

# Mission Leader
- Act as the primary liaison between the LASC Organization and the mission team for all mission-related topics.
- Manage and oversee all technical and non-technical aspects of the assigned mission.
- Ensure all deliverables, documentation, and technical reports are timely submitted.
- Coordinate with Safety, Operations, and Technology Managers to maintain mission readiness.
- Represent the mission in all technical discussions and reviews conducted by LASC officials.
- Initiate and handle all official communications with the LASC Organization regarding the mission.

# Safety Manager
- Ensure that the mission complies with the LASC Safety Policy throughout the entire project lifecycle, including design, development, testing, and on-site operations.
- Lead the creation and maintenance of the Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) and comprehensive Risk Assessments for the mission.
- Monitor and mitigate potential hazards during mission development and testing phases.
- Act as the mission's primary authority on safety-related matters and procedures.
- Support the team in identifying, documenting, and responding to potential nonconformities during the on-site event.
- Participate in the mission's safety reviews and ensure compliance with all applicable safety standards.
<!-- image -->

# Operations Manager
- Oversee and execute integrated testing and operational procedures for all components and systems of the experimental rocket.
- Ensure all applicable Latin American Space Challenge Rules and Policies (LSARPs) are correctly implemented in the vehicle.
- Serve as the primary point of contact for the LASC Launch Director and Operations Control staff during the on-site event.
- Coordinate and lead all mission activities related to the Launch Readiness Review (LRR) process.
- Ensure that the mission team is prepared and present to utilize its designated launch slot effectively and without delay.
- Support the Mission Leader in managing the schedule and logistics associated with launch operations.

# Technology Manager
- Ensure full compliance with all applicable Latin American Space Challenge Rules and Policies (LSARPs); develop and implement necessary technical features in the vehicle as required.
- Guarantee that the vehicle and its components meet the minimum performance standards for testing and launch operations.
- Prepare all materials required for the Presentation Session; responsible for the presentation if the mission is selected, in accordance with current rules.

# 1. Purpose
The Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) Event Guidance and Milestones (EGM) establishes the official guidelines for the event, including access and credentials, ticket fees, timelines, and the master schedule, to ensure effective participation in LASC.
This manual serves as the definitive reference for teams preparing for and participating in the competition, and shall be considered the primary source of information to ensure that all required submissions are completed on time.

# 3. LASC Documentation System
The Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) Documentation System is established by the LASC General Rules (LGR). Reading this document is essential to understand the structure of the documentation system and the concept of LASC Standards and Recommended Practices (LSARPs).

# 4. Manual Revisions
This document is subject to an annual revision, which invariably leads to a new edition of the EGM. In the event that critical issues emerge impacting the content, a revision to the current edition will be undertaken. All changes in this document are listed in the revision highlights table. For easier orientation, the following symbols identify any changes made within each section:
- Addition of a new item.
- Change to an item.
- Deletion of an item.

# Introduction
The 7th Edition of the Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) will take place in September 2026, in the cities of Bauru and Iacanga, located in the state of São Paulo, Brazil.
Day 1: The event begins with the LASC Conference, which will include safety inspections and evaluations of rockets and satellites, podium presentations, a group photo, keynote speeches, and networking opportunities. This will take place at a location to be announced.
Days 2 and 3: All activities will occur at the Sugarcane Launch Range, where launch and recovery operations will be carried out safely.
Day 4: The final launch and recovery windows will be held at the Sugarcane Launch Range. Later that day, the Awards Ceremony will take place at a location to be announced.

# Launch Site, Logistics & Communication
The Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) will be held at the Sugarcane Launch Range, a site that can safely support the growing number of teams and launches. Located in the cities of Iacanga and Bauru, in the state of São Paulo, Brazil, the Sugarcane Launch Range offers a safe environment and is close to hotels and other amenities.
Driving is a good option for transporting teams and rocket equipment. However, it is highly recommended to have more than one alert driver, and the front passenger should remain awake to assist the driver during the trip.

# International Teams
There are two main international airports near the region: São Paulo-Guarulhos International Airport (GRU) and Viracopos International Airport (VCP). When planning your trip, choose the safest and most reliable route to arrive in Brazil.
Note that GRU Airport is not in São Paulo city, but in the city of Guarulhos, about 15 km (around 1 hour by car) from the São Paulo city center. We do not recommend staying in Guarulhos or nearby towns.
Traveling from São Paulo or Guarulhos to Bauru/Iacanga with luggage and rocket-related materials can be challenging. Buses are available from Tietê Bus Terminal (São Paulo) to both cities. Teams must plan logistics in advance.

# Collaboration and Planning
International teams are encouraged to contact Brazilian teams for help with logistics and directions. All teams must collaborate and plan together before the event. Event officials will not
<!-- image -->
provide logistics support during the event. Teams that do not plan properly may face penalties or disqualification.

# Communication Channels
All official communications will be sent by email via lasc@lasc.space. Event officials are only required to respond to emails. Messages on WhatsApp, Discord, or other platforms are optional and not mandatory for officials.
Before the event, a WhatsApp group will be created for Team Leaders, Mission Leaders, and Safety Managers to help with urgent updates and quick communication.

# Documentation and Announcements
The LASC Documentation System and all required forms will be available on the LASC website: www.lasc.space. Important event updates will also be shared on social media, especially Instagram.

# Section 2 - Tickets, Fees, and Payments (PSP)
Section 2 defines the Tickets, Fees, and Payments (PSP) required for participation in the Latin American Space Challenge. While LASC is committed to promoting STEAM-related activities, it is still necessary to secure funding to ensure the event's successful execution.

# Explanation of Tickets and Associated Fees
The Latin American Space Challenge provides a vibrant and engaging environment supported by professional event infrastructure, including emergency services, launch facilities, and dedicated areas to ensure a safe and memorable experience for everyone involved.
Our staff and volunteers work tirelessly throughout the year to deliver this world-class event for students, sponsors, and spectators. Even if a team is not prepared to launch an experimental rocket or satellite, there are still valuable opportunities to present their projects and represent their institutions.
To make this extraordinary experience possible, fees are essential. Given the costs of delivering a comprehensive event, the following specific fees apply within the LASC ecosystem:
- Team Ticket: Each team must purchase one (1) Team Ticket, regardless of the number of selected missions or assigned tiers.
- Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket: Every team member attending the on-site event must purchase an individual Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket to gain access to restricted areas and to be eligible for a digital participation certificate.
- Spectator Ticket: Spectators are welcome to attend the Latin American Space Challenge on-site event. A designated Spectator Area will be available for parents, friends, and anyone interested in science, technology, and space activities.

- Spectator Ticket: Spectators are welcome to attend the Latin American Space Challenge on-site event. A designated Spectator Area will be available for parents, friends, and anyone interested in science, technology, and space activities.
If the event is canceled or postponed due to external factors beyond the LASC Organization's control (e.g. weather conditions), the Team Ticket will not be refundable. Rocketeer/Satelliteer Tickets will be 50% refundable if more than seven days have passed since the date of purchase. Refunds will follow each ticket's refund policy.
The following sections provide detailed explanations of each ticket type, along with the corresponding LSARP references applicable to all teams, missions, and participants.

# 2.1. Team Ticket
The Team Ticket is essential for the LASC Organization to cover upfront costs such as trophies, launch pads, essential services, and down payments for the event infrastructure prior to the on-site activities.
<!-- image -->
All teams are required to purchase one Team Ticket, regardless of the number of selected missions. However, the ticket price will be determined based on the operational complexity of the most advanced mission accepted for the team.
This revised pricing model aims to promote greater fairness by aligning costs with operational complexity, which takes into account the mission category, launch operations, and the infrastructure required.
PSP 2.1.1. All teams that have at least one Selected Mission, irrespective of tier assignment, are required to timely purchase only one Team Ticket. This requirement holds regardless of the number of missions submitted or challenges in which the team is participating.
The following table summarizes the Team Ticket pricing for each mission category, based on the assessed operational complexity of the selected missions.

Example 3: A team with two missions: 500 meters apogee with solid rocket propulsion system and PocketQube satellite project; will pay only USD 300 for the Team Ticket.
PSP 2.1.2. If a team fails to purchase the Team Ticket by the deadline established in Section 1.2 Administrative Deadlines, the team may forfeit its assigned tier and risk exclusion from the event. In the case of late payment, the Team Ticket price shall be doubled.

PSP 2.1.3. The Team Ticket is non-refundable, as event planning and infrastructure decisions are made based on the confirmed number of paying teams once payment is received.

PSP 2.1.3. The Team Ticket is non-refundable, as event planning and infrastructure decisions are made based on the confirmed number of paying teams once payment is received.
PSP 2.1.4. Teams with missions assigned as Tier 2 must purchase the Team Ticket as outlined in PSP 2.1.1. However, if a Tier 2 team completes check-in and receives a Launch Status of 'Approved', but is ultimately unable to launch due to a lack of available slots, the team will be eligible for a 50% refund of the Team Ticket. The refund will be processed within 30 (thirty) calendar days after the end of the event.
PSP 2.1.5. If a team with a Tier 2 mission is unable to complete launch operations due to external factors or circumstances such as inability to attend the event, technical issues occurring prior to the event, or receiving a Launch Status of 'Denied,' 'Action Needed,' or 'Provisional' as defined in Section 4.1 - Launch Readiness Review (LRR) of both RCSM and SCSM, the Team Ticket shall remain non-refundable.
PSP 2.1.6. If a team with multiple accepted missions withdraws its most operationally complex mission after the Mission Acceptance Announcement, the Team Ticket price shall remain unchanged and will not be recalculated based on the operational complexity of the remaining missions.

# 2.2. Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket
All team members willing to officially participate in the event shall purchase a Rocketeer & Satelliteer Ticket. This ticket includes access to the event and specific restricted areas, a kit containing a badge, lanyard and a promotional t-shirt, and a digital certificate of participation.
PSP 2.2.1. All rocketeers and satelliteers shall purchase a valid Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket. The ticket includes a badge, lanyard and a promotional t-shirt, and a digital certificate of participation.

# Important Notes
If an individual purchases a Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket but is unable to attend the on-site event, the LASC Organization will not ship or deliver the participant kit by mail or any other means. The kit will only be distributed during the event check-in.
Regarding Rocketeers and Satelliteers, Section 4.2, Access Control, establishes additional rules for access to improve security and event management, including the single-entry access per day to the Sugarcane Launch Range.
<!-- image -->
PSP 2.2.3. If an individual fails to purchase the Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket within the regular period, they may still acquire a 'Late' Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket by the final deadline specified in Section 1.2 -Administrative Deadlines. After this final deadline, no additional Rocketeer/Satelliteer Tickets will be available for purchase under any circumstances.
PSP 2.2.4. The Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket is personal and non-transferable. Participants shall not request or attempt any transfer of the ticket between individuals under any circumstances.
PSP 2.2.5. A participant holding a valid Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket may request a full refund within 7 (seven) calendar days from the date of purchase. To do so, the participant must contact the LASC Staff by emailing lasc@lasc.space with the subject line: 'Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket Refund'. Refund processing may take up to 30 (thirty) calendar days.

PSP 2.1.6. All team members must hold a valid ticket issued by the Latin American Space Challenge to complete check-in and access the on-site event, including restricted areas. If an individual without a valid Rocketeer/Satelliteer Ticket (e.g., not wearing an appropriate Official Badge) is found within a restricted area, the entire team may be disqualified from the event.

# Ticket Purchase by Public Process ( Only for Brazilian Teams )
Brazilian teams supported by public institutions or universities that intend to purchase the Team Ticket through a public procurement process in accordance with Lei nº 14.133, de 1º de abril de 2021, must plan accordingly due to the administrative steps required to complete the process.
A public procurement process is a time-consuming activity that demands significant time and effort from the LASC staff. Each request involves the preparation of documentation, proposals, coordination of meetings, and other administrative tasks to ensure the successful completion of the process. Teams must plan accordingly and initiate the process well in advance to avoid delays or complications.
PSP 2.1.7. If a Brazilian team intends to purchase any type of ticket through a public procurement process, the team leader must plan accordingly and contact the LASC Staff at least 45 (forty-five) days prior to the ticket purchase deadline by sending an email to lasc@lasc.space with the subject line: '[Team Name] - Purchase by Public Process'.

# Spectator Ticket
The Latin American Space Challenge warmly welcomes all spectators. Family members, friends, and the entire community are invited to join and experience the event.
Since its inception, LASC has actively encouraged community participation, becoming a benchmark for events such as IREC. We take pride in providing broad access to STEAM-related activities, fostering the growth and promotion of the space sector.
<!-- image -->
The Spectator Ticket is valid for all days of the event. All spectators with a valid ticket will receive an Official Badge during the first check-in, which must be worn throughout the event. There will be no badge replacement.

# Parking Ticket

All other vehicles may be subject to parking fees at the Sugarcane Launch Range. Details regarding these fees will be communicated on the day prior to the event.

# Section 3 - Event Infrastructure (EIF)
Section 3 defines the Event Infrastructure (EIF) for the onsite event. The Latin American Space Challenge features two locations: the Ginásio de Esportes Beira Rio and the Sugarcane Launch Range (SLR). During the event days, roadblocks may be in place. Teams should plan and arrange transportation in advance.

The Ginásio de Esportes Beira Rio is located at the Av. São João, 360-482 Iacanga - SP. The location is 51 km from Bauru city and 397 km from the São Paulo-Guarulhos International Airport (GRU).
The Google Maps link with correct position of the Ginásio de Esportes Beira Rio can be accessed by the following link: https://maps.app.goo.gl/WruRmtMyvJAD7xpL7

<!-- image -->
The Ginásio de Esportes Beira Rio will provide basic infrastructure for teams to check in and register for the event, as outlined in the EGM document.
All teams will have their rockets and/or satellites evaluated on the first day. Selected teams may present their projects to the general audience during the Podium Session.
During the event, tables will be available for each team to display their projects. No electricity will be provided for teams or participants, so plan accordingly. The venue includes bathrooms.
EIF 3.1.1 Bringing any type of energetics or propellant to the Ginásio de Esportes Beira Rio is strictly prohibited.
<!-- image -->

# 3.2. Sugarcane Launch Range (SLR)
The launch site is located at the Sugarcane Launch Range (SLR) , approximately 50 km from Bauru and 10 km from Iacanga. The site is on private property, and all access must comply with the LASC documents and approvals.
EIF 3.2.1 Access to the Sugarcane Launch Range is strictly prohibited without prior written approval from LASC Staff. Violation of this rule may result in disqualification and permanent exclusion from future LASC events.
The Google Maps link with correct position of the Sugarcane Launch Range (SLR) can be accessed by the following link: https://maps.app.goo.gl/EC2pqGwvyXwBRYmJ6
Figure 2 - Route from Bauru and Iacanga to the Sugarcane Launch Range (SLR).
<!-- image -->
Access to the Sugarcane Launch Range is regulated in Section 4.2 of this document. The site infrastructure is divided into Public Areas and Restricted Areas.
- Non-restricted Areas: Parking Lot, Main Entrance, Event Center, and Spectator Area.
- Restricted Areas: Rocket Assembly Area (RAA), Pyro Preparation Area (PPA), Mission Control Center (MCC), Launch Control Center (LCC), and Launch Pads.
<!-- image -->

# Parking
Cars and vans shall be parked at the Parking Lot. Only Staff Vehicles will be authorized to drive to the Event Center and other areas.
Buses will be permitted to park at the SLR, but there will be a limit of parking spots due to infrastructure limitations. All teams arriving by bus must plan for embarkation and disembarkation at the SLR entrance. Buses must not park on the highway.
Important note: The distance between the SLR entrance and the Event Center is about 800 meters.

# Non-restricted Areas
The Non-restricted Areas primarily consist of the Parking Lot, Event Center, and Spectator Area, where both spectators and team members can access a truss tent structure. This space will be open for leisure activities, live event streaming, presentations, safety briefings, and ad-hoc announcements. A food court with accessible food and drinks will be available in the area.

# Restricted Areas
Access to the Rocket Assembly Area (RAA) will be controlled by the Mission Control Center (MCC). Only registered Rocketeers and Satelliteers will be permitted to enter the area.
The Launch Control Center (LCC) and Launch Pads are restricted zones, and entry is prohibited unless authorized by an Operations Control official.
The minimum distance from any launch pad at the SLR to any person or property not associated with the operation will be no less than 320 meters or 1,050 ft, in accordance with the Tripoli Rocketry Association Safety Code.
The minimum distance from any launch pad at the SLR to any person or property associated with the operation will be no less than 800 meters.
It is defined as a 'person associated with the operation' of all LASC Operations Control Staff and the maximum of 1 (one) operator of each team clearly authorized by the LASC Operations Control official.
Note: For Hybrids and Liquids, the total number of operators will be defined by the LASC Operations Control official.
<!-- image -->

# Section 4 - Logistics and Event Access (LES)
Section 3 defines the Logistics and Access (LES) for the onsite event. All teams and team members shall comply with the applicable LSARPs in this section at all times throughout the duration of the onsite event.Failure to comply with the provisions outlined herein may result in penalties applied to the entire team.

# 4.1. Event Check-in
LES 4.1.1. All participants of the Latin American Space Challenge shall present an acceptable form of government-issued photo identification to access the Sugarcane Launch Range. The following documents will be accepted during check-in:
- Valid Passport
- National ID Card
- Driver's license
Failure to present a valid document will result in the denial of the team member's badge issuance.
LES 4.1.2 Event Check-in will take place as informed in Section 1.3 of this document. Attendance is mandatory for the Team Leader or the Operations Manager, accompanied by no more than three (3) additional team members.
Note: During check-in, valid identification documents for all team members shall be presented in order to collect their official badges.

# 4.2. Access Control
Team members will receive their event badges during the official Event Check-in, after the Team Leader or Operations Manager collects them with the appropriate official documentation, as previously described.
LES 4.2.1 Any individual arriving after the official Event Check-in period shall check in and collect their badges at the Onsite Registration and Check-in location, situated at the main entrance of the event venue.
LES 4.1.2 All Rocketeers and Satelliteers shall be granted a single-entry access per day to the Sugarcane Launch Range. If a team member exits the event grounds, re-entry on the same day will not be permitted under any circumstances. Participants are strongly advised to plan accordingly to avoid any inconvenience related to this restriction.

# 4.3. Badges
There are four badge categories: (1) Spectator, (2) Rocketeer/Satelliteer, (3) Staff, and (4) Operations Control. Each badge category corresponds to a specific access level and grants
<!-- image -->
permission to enter designated areas of the event, including, where applicable, the launch pad. Additionally, each badge clearly identifies the role and responsibilities of the individual wearing it throughout the event.
LES 4.3.1 All participants shall wear their badge in a clearly visible manner at all times while within the event premises.

# 'Spectator' Wristband Personnel
Friends and family of LASC Rocketeers/Satelliteers, and members of the general public are welcome to come to spectate the launch. These individuals will be asked to pay a spectator fee at the entrance to the event in exchange for an official 'Spectator' Wristband.
This wristband grants them access onto the Sugarcane Launch Range, and unescorted access within the designated Public Area.

# 'Rocketeer/Satelliteer' Badged Personnel
All Rocketeers and Satelliteers will be issued a themed badge labeled "Rocketeer/Satelliteer". This badge grants: (1) Access to the Sugarcane Launch Range, and (2) Unescorted access to both the Public Area and the Rocket Assembly Area.
During launch operations, only authorized Rocketeers and Satelliteers may be granted access to the Launch Area(s), in accordance with the Launch Director (LD) and Range Safety Officer (RSO)'s latest instructions.

# 'Staff' Badged Personnel
All LASC Staff and Competition Officials will be issued a themed badge labeled "Staff". This badge grants: (1) Access to the Sugarcane Launch Range, and (2) Unescorted access to the designated Public Area, and Rocket Assembly Area (RAA).
During launch operations, Staff personnel may be granted access to the Launch Area(s) in accordance with the latest instructions issued by the Launch Director (LD) and the Range Safety Officer (RSO).

# 'Operations Control' Badged Personnel
Members of the Operations Control team will be issued a themed badge labeled "Operations Control". Important:
This badge grants unrestricted access to all areas of the Sugarcane Launch Range. Operations Control personnel are responsible for overseeing launch operations.
<!-- image -->

# 4.4. Vehicle and Transportation
LES 4.4.1 All vehicles entering the Sugarcane Launch Range shall undergo a check-in process. Fees may apply depending on the type of vehicle. All vehicles must be parked in the designated Parking Lot and, if asked to, display a valid Vehicle Placard to be permitted to exit the location.
LES 4.4.2 If requested by LASC Officials, specific placards shall be displayed prominently either on top of the dashboard or hanging from the rearview mirror.
LES 4.4.3 Teams are responsible for arranging their own transportation to and from LASC, as well as for their mobility during the event.
LES 4.4.4 If a team requires loading or unloading logistics, temporary access to the Rocket Assembly Area (RAA) may be granted, subject to prior approval from the LASC Operations Control official present onsite. Outside of such approved activities, teams shall comply with the designated parking regulations at all times.
LES 4.4.5 No vehicle may operate at a speed exceeding 30 km/h within the Sugarcane Launch Range, and must not exceed 10 km/h when operating in close proximity to personnel or equipment.
LES 4.4.6 Any person found operating a vehicle in an unsafe manner shall have their on-site vehicle privileges revoked, and may be subject to immediate removal from the Sugarcane Launch Range, depending on the severity and/or recurrence of the infraction.

LES 4.4.6 Any person found operating a vehicle in an unsafe manner shall have their on-site vehicle privileges revoked, and may be subject to immediate removal from the Sugarcane Launch Range, depending on the severity and/or recurrence of the infraction.
LES 4.4.7 Access to the Launch Area of the Sugarcane Launch Range is restricted to authorized personnel only. All individuals must carry and present their event credentials, provided during registration, at all times.

# 4.5. General Provisions
The following provisions establish the baseline safety rules and outline key logistical guidelines applicable throughout the entire event. All participants, staff, and attendees are expected to comply strictly with these provisions to ensure a safe, organized, and successful event.

# Prohibited Conduct Violation
Violation of any of the provisions outlined in this section or any other critical safety, security, or conduct-related regulations may result in the immediate removal of the individual from the Sugarcane Launch Range.
Depending on the severity and nature of the violation, penalties may also include the disqualification of the individual's associated team from the current competition and potential exclusion from future editions of the Latin American Space Challenge (LASC).
<!-- image -->
LES 4.5.1 The possession of any kind of weapon-whether open or concealed-is strictly prohibited by all participants, spectators, and staff throughout the entire duration of the event and within all areas of the event venue, including both public and restricted zones.
LES 4.5.2 Smoking and the consumption or possession of alcoholic beverages are strictly prohibited within all areas of the Sugarcane Launch Range during the entire duration of the event. The presence of any open container of alcoholic beverages will result in the immediate removal of the individual from the premises.

LES 4.5.3 The use, possession, distribution, or trafficking of any illegal drugs or controlled substances, as defined by Brazilian law, is strictly prohibited at any time and in any location associated with the Latin American Space Challenge (LASC), including but not limited to the Sugarcane Launch Range, Public Areas, and Restricted Areas. LASC Organization reserves the right to report the incident to the appropriate legal authorities.
LES 4.5.4 Any form of verbal or physical aggression, threats, or misconduct by any participant shall be considered a serious violation of conduct and may result in disciplinary action, including immediate removal from the event and disqualification of the associated team.

# Camping, Cooking, and Trash Disposal
LES 4.5.5 LASC participants are prohibited to camp onsite at the Sugarcane Launch Range Area.
LES 4.5.6 Cooking of any kind is not permitted within the Sugarcane Launch Range. The use of charcoal, open flames, or any cooking equipment is strictly prohibited.
LES 4.5.7 All participants shall not bring any type of food to the event, unless previously authorized by the LASC Organization. The event will provide accessible food trucks and other food vendors to ensure the availability of meals and refreshments throughout the event at the Sugarcane Launch Range Area.
LES 4.5.8 All attendees are responsible for preventing littering. All trash will be disposed of in provided waste receptacles or bagged by the participants themselves, and disposed of at the indicated areas. Chemical latrine facilities (i.e., 'porta-potties') sufficient for roughly 1,500 attendees will be provided and serviced every day.

# Recommended and Required Dress
LES 4.5.9 All attendees will dress appropriately for the harsh environment - including sun exposure, terrain, and wildlife - by using the following guidance:
- Attendees should protect themselves from sunburn, reapplied regularly as directed by the manufacturer;
- Attendees should protect themselves from eye damage due to sun exposure with UV protection sunglasses;
<!-- image -->
- Attendees are required to wear closed toe footwear - which may include closed toe sandals - at all times in the Rocket Assembly Area (RAA) and Launch Area, as well as while engaged in rocket recovery. No individual wearing open toe sandals will be permitted to engage in rocket recovery.
- Attendees are required to wear closed pants at all times in the RAA and Launch area.
Additional information is also available in specific sections of the RCSM and on the LASC website.

# Propellant
LES 4.6.1. All teams are solely and entirely responsible for the acquisition, handling, preparation, testing, transportation, and storage of any type of propellant used in their missions. These activities must be conducted in strict compliance with all applicable municipal, state, and federal laws and regulations, including but not limited to those established by the Brazilian Army, and other relevant authorities.
The Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) Organization shall not be held responsible or liable under any circumstances for incidents, damages, injuries, or legal consequences resulting from the handling, use, or presence of propellants by participating teams.
During the on-site event, no team is allowed to handle, integrate, or seal any loaded rocket motor without obtaining prior formal authorization from the LASC Organization in accordance with the RCSM. Failure to comply may result in immediate disqualification and removal from the event, as well as potential notification to competent legal authorities.

# Liquids and Gases
LES 4.6.1. All liquid and gaseous propellants must be acquired, stored, and managed by each team independently. The Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) Organization shall not be responsible for providing, facilitating, or coordinating the acquisition, transportation, or logistics of such materials.
LES 4.6.2. All cylinders or storage vessels containing gases and/or liquids intended for use during launch operations must conform to recognized international safety standards, such as ISO 9809 or equivalent.
LES 4.6.3. Each team is fully responsible for coordinating the transport, delivery, and retrieval of cylinders and associated consumables with third-party suppliers and event officials. Under no circumstances will the LASC Organization receive or store cylinders on behalf of any team at the launch range before the first day of the event.
LES 4.6.4. All cylinders must be equipped with appropriate trolleys for safe ground transportation. Manual handling or the use of non-compliant transport methods is strictly prohibited.
<!-- image -->
LES 4.6.4. Teams are responsible for ordering and securing a sufficient quantity of propellants and oxidizers to account for potential mishaps or additional launch attempts. No reserve or emergency stock will be maintained by the LASC Organization.
Additionally, teams must ensure compatibility of bottle fittings with local suppliers and make all necessary preparations to avoid operational disruptions.

# Energetics
LES 4.6.5. All other energetics, such as black powder, igniters, CO₂ cartridges, and similar items, must also be acquired and managed by the participating teams. The LASC Organization shall not provide, store, or facilitate access to any such energetic materials.

# 4.7. Import and Export Guidance
SRAD hybrid and liquid motors may generally be imported into Brazil in a neutral, non-hazardous state. However, it is the sole responsibility of each team to ensure full compliance with all applicable Brazilian import regulations. This includes securing any required authorizations, licenses, and documentation prior to shipment. The same requirements apply to inert propellants intended for use with hybrid motors.
LES 4.7.1 If shipping components via parcel or freight, teams shall plan for adequate lead times. Delays may occur due to customs clearance procedures, inspections, or other logistical complications, which may compromise a team's ability to participate in launch activities.
LES 4.7.2 It is strictly prohibited for teams to directly import loaded SRAD solid motors or any type of SRAD propellant into Brazil. International teams are strongly encouraged to seek partnerships or support from local universities, companies, or other LASC-participating teams to procure propellants, energetics, and/or fluids required for SRAD solid, hybrid, or liquid propulsion systems.
LES 4.7.3. It is strictly forbidden for the teams to directly import loaded COTS solid motors into Brazil.
LES 4.7.4 Any solid motors, energetics, and/or fluids remaining unused at the conclusion of the event will be subject to destruction unless teams arrange a legally compliant export method or propose another feasible and approved alternative in advance.

# 4.9. Use of Handheld Radios
Teams are permitted to use handheld two -way radios suitable for onsite coordination at the Sugarcane Launch Range. The recommended models are Baofeng BF -888S and similar (single -band UHF analog FM handheld with programmable channels).
Equivalent low-power devices with comparable functionality are permitted, provided they do not interfere with reserved event channels or frequencies pertaining to systems telemetry, or WiFi.
LES 4.9.1 Teams shall not transmit or receive on any radio channels designated for exclusive use by LASC Staff or Operations Control. These channels will be communicated prior and/or during the event. Teams may use any other available frequencies, provided that such use does not cause interference with operations or violate applicable regulations.
LES 4.9.2 Each team should maintain at least one handheld radio powered on at all times on the event -wide monitoring channel. This channel will be used for operational announcements, safety notices, and launch countdowns.
<!-- image -->
LES 4.9.3 Teams should carry at least one handheld radio configured to the designated recovery channel during all rocket recovery operations. This ensures that teams remain reachable for safety coordination and status updates while away from the launch area.

<!-- image -->
LES 4.9.3 Teams should carry at least one handheld radio configured to the designated recovery channel during all rocket recovery operations. This ensures that teams remain reachable for safety coordination and status updates while away from the launch area.
LES 4.9.4 All teams should adhere to ANATEL regulations, including restrictions on transmitting in frequency bands reserved for amateur radio operators, which require proper licensing under Brazilian law. Teams are responsible for ensuring that their chosen frequencies, power levels, and equipment use remain compliant with national radio regulations.
LES 4.9.5 Teams are fully responsible for programming, labeling, charging, and maintaining their radios. Sufficient spare batteries, power banks, or charging provisions are strongly recommended.

# 1. Introduction
The Mission Acceptance Announcement for the Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) is issued in accordance with the guidelines established in the LASC Mission Selection Process, which forms part of the official Application and Registration Process.
All submitted missions were subjected to a rigorous evaluation conducted by an expert committee in order to determine their acceptance status and appropriate classification into Tier 1 or Tier 2. The selection process was based on a comprehensive set of criteria designed to maximize operational safety and overall project quality.
These criteria included compliance with the LASC Documentation System as defined in the LASC General Rules (LGR), as well as the quality and completeness of the submitted mission documentation, the level of technological innovation, and the team's experience, history, and maturity based on participation in previous editions of the event.
For the 7th Edition of the Latin American Space Challenge, a Slot System has been implemented to further enhance the safety, organization, and professionalism across the event. Accepted missions are therefore divided into two tiers, as described below:
- Tier 1 - Selected Missions : Tier 1 missions will be assigned and have reserved presentation and/or launch slots during the onsite event, in accordance with the event rules and operational schedule.

- Tier 1 - Selected Missions : Tier 1 missions will be assigned and have reserved presentation and/or launch slots during the onsite event, in accordance with the event rules and operational schedule.
- Tier 2 - Selected Missions: Tier 2 missions will not have guaranteed presentation and/or launch slots during the onsite event. However, they may be allocated vacant, canceled, or newly available slots during the event, subject to operational availability and organizational discretion.
The LASC Organization reserves the right to unilaterally change the tier status of any mission at any time without prior notice if deemed necessary for safety, operational, or organizational reasons. Notwithstanding the submission or non-submission of the confirmation form, all selected missions remain listed in this Acceptance Announcement document.

# Record of Revisions
The Rocket Challenge Standards Manual (RCSM), previously known as the LASC Rules & Requirements Document, has been revised to streamline the documentation system by consolidating information into a single document. As a result, the rules and requirements for mission organization, as well as the technical specifications for the experimental rocket, are now available in this document.
