# Section 5 - CubeSat Requirements (CUB)
Section 5 defines the specific requirements for CubeSat (CUB) satellite projects, including form-factor dimensions, mass, specific guidance, and other related criteria.

# 5.1. CubeSat Mass, Volume, and Interface Requirements
CubeSats are nanosatellite platforms based on a cubic-shaped form factor (approximately 100x100x100 mm per unit).
The external dimensions and typical maximum mass are slightly different depending on the number of units, as outlined in the following for the 1U, 2U and 3U cases.
CUB 5.1.1. The only CubeSat form-factors permitted for the Latin American Space Challenge are 1U, 2U, and 3U.
Table 2. CubeSat Dimensions.
| Number of Units (U) | External Dimensions (mm) | Minimum Mass (g) | Maximum Mass (g) |
|-----------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1U | 100x100x100 | 700 | 1330 |
| 200x100x100 | | 1400 | 2U 2660 |
| 3U 300x100x100 | 2800 | | 4000 |
CUB 5.1.2. Deployables such as antennas shall be constrained by the CubeSat, not the dispenser.
CUB 5.1.3. The CubeSat center of gravity should fall within the ranges specified in the following table:
Table 3: Ranges of Acceptable CG Locations as Measured from the Geometric Center on each Axis.
| Number of Units (U) | X axis (cm) | Y axis (cm) | Z axis (cm) |
|-----------------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| + 2 / -2 | + 2 / -2 | + 2 / -2 | 1U |
| + 2 / -2 | + 2 / -2 | + 4.5 / -4.5 | 2U |
| + 2 / -2 | + 2 / -2 | + 7 / -7 | 3U

| Number of Units (U) | X axis (cm) | Y axis (cm) | Z axis (cm) |
|-----------------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| + 2 / -2 | + 2 / -2 | + 2 / -2 | 1U |
| + 2 / -2 | + 2 / -2 | + 4.5 / -4.5 | 2U |
| + 2 / -2 | + 2 / -2 | + 7 / -7 | 3U |
CUB 5.1.4. The CubeSat structure should be made from aluminum alloy. Typically, Aluminum 7075, 6061, 6082, 5005, and/or 5052 are used for both the main CubeSat structure and the rails.
CUB 5.1.5. Rails should have a minimum width of 8.5 mm measured from the edge of the rail to the first protrusion on each face. Rails should have a surface roughness less than 1.6 µm. The edges of the rails should be rounded to a radius of at least 1 mm. The ends of the rails on the +/Z face shall have a minimum surface area of 6.5 mm x 6.5 mm contact area with neighboring CubeSat rails.
<!-- image -->

# Section 6 - CanSat Requirements (CAN)
Section 6 defines the specific requirements for CanSat (CAN) satellite projects, including form-factor dimensions, mass, specific guidance, and other related criteria.

# 6.1. CanSat Mass, Volume, and Interface Requirements
CanSats are picosatellite platforms based on a cylinder-shaped form factor. The challenge is to fit all the major subsystems, such as power, sensors and a communication system, into this minimal volume.
CAN 6.1.1. The only permitted CanSat form-factor for the Latin American Space Challenge is 1C, as described in Table 4. All missions with a CanSat project shall comply with this established form factor.
Table 4. CanSat Dimensions.
| Form Factor | Diameter (mm) | Height (mm) | Minimum Mass (g) | Maximum Mass (g) |
|---------------|-----------------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1C | 66 | 105 | 300 | 400 |
CAN 6.1.2. The mass of the CanSat must be between a minimum of 200g and a maximum of 300g. CanSats that are lighter must take additional ballast with them to reach the 200g minimum mass lower-limit required.
CAN 6.1.3. Radio antennas and GPS antennas can be mounted externally on the top or bottom of the can, depending on the design, but not on the sides.
CAN 6.1.4. Any antennas, transducers and other elements of the CanSat cannot extend beyond the can's diameter until it has left the launch vehicle.
CAN 6.1.5. Recommended materials for the structure are: PLA, ABS, PP e PETG. Potential metallic materials such as aluminum alloy may also be used.
<!-- image -->

# Section 7 - PocketQube Requirements (PQB)
Section 7 defines the specific requirements for PocketQube (PQB) satellite projects, including form-factor dimensions, mass, specific guidance, and other related criteria.

# 7.1. PocketQube Mass, Volume, and Interface Requirements
PocketQubes are picosatellite platforms based on a cubic-shaped form factor (approximately 50x50x50 mm per unit) with each side one-half smaller than a CubeSat (100x100x100 mm per unit).
PQB 7.1.1. The only PocketQube form-factors permitted for the Latin American Space Challenge are 1P, 2P, and 3P.
Table 5. PocketQube dimensions.
| Number of Units (P) | External Dimensions without Backplate (mm) | Sliding Backplate Dimension (mm) |
|-----------------------|----------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| 50x50x50 | 58x64x1.6 | 1P |
| 50x50x114 | 58x128x1.6 | 2P |
| 50x50x178 | 58x192x1.6 | 3P |
PQB 7.1.2. PocketQube-projects shall comply with the established minimum and maximum required mass detailed in Table 6.
Table 6. PocketQube minimum and maximum mass.
| Number of Units (P) | Minimum Mass (g) | Maximum Mass (g) |
|-----------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 200 | 250 | 1P |
| 400 | 500 | 2P |
| 600 | 750 | 3P

| Maximum Mass (g) |
|-----------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 200 | 250 | 1P |
| 400 | 500 | 2P |
| 600 | 750 | 3P |
PQB 7.1.3. The envelope around the PocketQube shall be no more than 7 mm for components.
PQB 7.1.4. For appendages and deployables, maximum 10 mm is allowed. All deployable components shall be constrained by the PocketQube and not by the deployer.
PQB 7.1.5. The center of mass of the PocketQube shall not exceed 1 cm from its geometric center in a stowed position.
PQB 7.1.6. Recommended materials for the baseplate are: FR4 and Aluminum (7075, 6061, 6065, 6082).
<!-- image -->
PQB 7.1.7. Recommended materials for the structure are: PLA, ABS, PP e PETG. Potential metallic materials such as aluminum alloy may also be used.
PQB 7.1.8. Potential metallic materials used for the PocketQube that are in contact with the deployer and standoffs should be hard anodized.
PQB 7.1.9. All PocketQubes shall use at least two kill switches to keep the satellite offline while in the deployer.
PQB 7.1.10. Kill switches shall be located only on the Z-axis. There are two different possible placement areas:
- a) In the lateral side of the satellite within 20 mm from the Z-faces and touching the deployment rails;
- b) Aligned with the sliding backplate in Z-face and in contact with the PocketQube below or the pusher plate.
PQB 7.1.11. The kill switches shall not obstruct ejection of the satellite from the deployer and/or experimental rocket.

# Section 8 - Satellite Structure and Materials (SSM)
Section 6 defines the Satellite Structure and Materials (SSM) overall guidance and requirements for the development of satellite projects, including material selection and prohibitions.
The following provisions address some key points applicable to the satellite projects, but are not exhaustive of the conditions affecting each unique design. Each team is responsible for thoroughly understanding, analyzing, and mitigating their design's unique load set.

# 8.1. Overall Structural Integrity
STR 8.1.1. Satellites shall be constructed to withstand the operating stresses and retain structural integrity under the conditions encountered during handling transportation, and the potential launch operation.

# 8.2. Material Selection and Tests
STR 8.2.1. Satellites shall be constructed from lightweight materials, such as 3D-printed low-temperature polymers or ductile lightweight metals like aluminum. The choice of materials and assembly techniques must be appropriate for the specific requirements to ensure optimal performance and safety.
STR 8.2.2. The use of natural composites such as wood, cardboard, hemp fiber composites, bamboo composites, etc. should be examined for effectiveness and appropriateness.
STR 8.2.3. Teams should simulate and/or test the properties of materials and the strength of components developed for use in their satellites. This process should include both individual and joint analyses to ensure the integrity of all parts.
It is important to verify the suitability and durability of all materials and parts under the specific conditions they will face during their missions, as this is essential for ensuring launch safety.
STR 8.2.4. Environmentally hazardous materials shall not be used in the satellite project. This includes lead, mercury, uranium, and any other radioactive materials.
<!-- image -->

# Section 9 - Electronic Power System and Communications (ECS)
Section 9 defines the Electronic Power System and Communications (ECS) guidance and overall requirements for any satellite mission.

# Electronic Power Systems and Rail Standby Time
ECS 9.1.1. The on-board power system shall be designed and provided by each team. Teams are not required to design solar panels.
ECS 9.1.2. The on-board power system shall store enough energy to ensure power throughout the test mission or launch campaign.
ECS 9.1.3. In order to overcome critically inefficient use of valuable and limited launch campaign time, including slot availability, satellite projects shall have at least 4 (four) hours of battery endurance.
ECS 9.1.4. Satellites should incorporate battery circuit protection for charging/discharging to avoid unbalanced cell conditions.

# Remove Before Flight (RBF)
ECS 9.1.5. Each satellite shall include an RBF pin.
ECS 9.1.6. The RBF pin shall cut all power to the satellite once it is inserted into the satellite.
ECS 9.1.7. The RBF pin shall be removed from the satellite after integration into the experimental launch vehicle.

# 9.2. Communications and Radio Frequency
ECS 9.2.1. The communication system shall be designed and provided by each team.
ECS 9.2.2. The communication system shall be wireless.
ECS 9.2.3. The satellite's communication system should be capable of establishing reliable communication, and, if necessary, command and control the experimental satellite from an isolated location.
Telemetry, Tracking, and Control (TT&C) systems represent a foundational element in the architecture of next-generation satellites. LASC encourages all participating missions to implement TT&C modules in their experimental satellite designs.
<!-- image -->
ECS 9.2.4. All wireless communications should use U.S. or Brazilian legal frequencies and shall be used responsibly

# Radio Frequency
ECS 9.2.5. Competitors agree that use of Radio Frequencies (RF) for any purpose will be in accordance with U.S. or Brazilian laws and regulations. Each team shall coordinate frequency allocation with the LASC Organizers.
ECS 9.2.6. For all communications, any electromagnetic spectrum frequency (e.g., RF, infrared, visible light, etc.) is allowed, subject to all applicable spectrum allocation.
ECS 9.2.7. Each team is responsible for coordinating RF operating licenses for both their satellite and for all ground stations under their control if required.
<!-- image -->

# Section 10 - Recovery System (REC)
Section 10 defines the Recovery System (REC) guidance for the recovery system, parachute design, and other requirements.

# 10.1. Guidance
Satellites may remain secured within the payload bay of the experimental rocket and be recovered together with it, or may be independently recovered (e.g., using their own parachute or alternative recovery systems).
REC 10.1.1. The satellite shall be independent of the experimental rocket, with no fixed electrical connection between the two. The satellite shall not command any deployment action of the recovery system or any other type of action.
REC 10.1.2. If a dedicated recovery mission is implemented for the satellite, the satellite team shall plan and define in advance whether the recovery will be conducted using a dependent or independent recovery system.
REC 10.1.3. Satellite missions with a dependent recovery system shall keep the satellite inside the payload bay or airframe throughout the entire launch operation.
REC 10.1.4. If a satellite mission opts for an independent recovery system, the size, mass, and volume of the recovery system shall not be included in the satellite's form factor calculations and measurements.
REC 10.1.5. If a satellite mission decides to use an independent recovery system, the parachute or non-parachute recovery device shall be easily detachable from the structure.

# Deployment Event for Satellites with Independent Recovery System
REC 10.1.5. The deployment event for independent payload/satellite recovery shall occur at or lower than 1,500 meters above ground level, with a target descent rate sufficient to permit data collection but not so high as to exacerbate wind drift (e.g., between 20-45 m/s).
REC 10.1.6. The satellite shall be protected to prevent damage from the deployment event initiated by the experimental rocket's recovery electronic system.

# 10.2. Parachute Design Guidance
It is highly recommended that teams choose traditional parachute-based recovery systems. The majority of flight failures occur during recovery, so keeping it simple is advisable, especially if part of the launch operations score directly or indirectly depends on the successful recovery of the satellite.
<!-- image -->

# Non-Parachute/Parafoil Recovery Systems
REC 10.2.1. Teams exploring other (i.e., non-parachute or parafoil based) recovery methods shall notify LASC of their intentions at the earliest possible opportunity, and keep LASC apprised of the situation as their work progresses. LASC may make additional requests for information and draft unique requirements depending on the team's specific design implementation.

# Ejection Gas Protection
REC 10.2.2. The recovery system shall implement adequate protection (e.g., fire resistant material, pistons, baffles) to prevent hot ejection gasses (if implemented) from causing burn damage to retaining cords and other vital components as the specific design demands.

# Parachute Coloration And Markings
REC 10.2.3. Parachutes shall be high-contrast to the local environment. It is recommended to use orange, white, and/or red colors. This will enable ground-based observers to more easily characterize deployment events with high-power optics..
<!-- image -->

# Abstract
The Mission Report shall contain an Abstract. At a minimum, the abstract shall identify the mission category in which the team is competing, identify any unique/defining design characteristics of the project, and provide whatever additional information may be necessary to convey any other high-level project or program goals & objectives.

# Introduction
The Mission Report shall contain an Introduction. This section provides an overview of the academic program, stakeholders, team structure, and mission organization and management strategies. The introduction may repeat some of the content included in the abstract, because the abstract is intended to act as a standalone synopsis if necessary.

# System Architecture and Concept of Operations
The Mission Report shall contain a System Architecture Overview. This part shall begin with a top-level overview of the integrated system, including a cutaway figure depicting the fully integrated project and its major subsystems or modules - configured for the mission proposed.
The Mission Report shall contain a Mission Concept of Operations (ConOps) Overview. This part shall identify the mission phases, including a figure, and describe the nominal operation of all subsystems or modules during each phase (e.g., a description of what is supposed to be occurring in each phase, and what subsystems are responsible for accomplishing this).
No matter how a team defines mission phases and phase transitions, they will be used to help organize failure modes identified in a Risk Assessment Appendix - described in this document.

# Weights, Measures, and Performance Data
The Mission Report shall contain Weights, Measures, and Performance Data. This requirement will be satisfied by informing the data in this section of the document. A table with the minimum data is available in the Mission Report template.

# Conclusions and Lessons Learned
The Mission Report shall contain Conclusions and Lessons Learned. This section shall include the lessons learned during the design, manufacture, and testing of the project, both from a team management and technical development perspective. Furthermore, this section should include strategies for corporate knowledge transfer from senior team members to the rising underclassmen who will soon take their place.

# Appendix A: Details for the Mission Report
<!-- image -->

# Hazard Analysis Appendix
The first Mission Report appendix shall contain a Hazard Analysis Report. This appendix shall address as applicable, hazardous material handling, transportation, storage procedures, and any other aspects of the design which pose potential hazards to operating personnel. A mitigation approach - by process and/or design - shall be defined for each hazard identified.

# Risk Assessment Appendix
The second Mission Report appendix shall contain a Risk Assessment. This appendix shall summarize risk and reliability concepts associated with the project. All identified failure modes which pose a risk to mission success shall be recorded in a matrix, organized according to the mission phases identified by the ConOps.
A mitigation approach - by process and/or design - shall be defined for each risk identified. A common description of the Risk Assessment is FMECA (Failure Mode and Effect Criticality Analysis). A Risk Assessment/FMECA is often represented as a spreadsheet matrix. The input to the matrix is listed as follows:
- A description of the identified failure mode.
- The likelihood of the failure mode occurring.
- The severity and impact of the failure mode occurring
The likelihood of a failure mode occurrence and the severity of the occurrence is assigned values according to the following tables:
Table 7: Likelihood of Failure.
| Failure Probability | Value Assessment of Risk |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Remote 1 | This is unlikely to happen. |
| 2 | Occasional This might happen. |
| Probable or likely | 3 This is likely to happen. |
Table 8: Severity of Occurrence.
| Mishap Severity | Value | Effect of Failure Mode |
|-------------------|-------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| or | Minor impact on mission. | Minor negligible 1 |
| 2

| 3 This is likely to happen. |
Table 8: Severity of Occurrence.
| Mishap Severity | Value | Effect of Failure Mode |
|-------------------|-------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| or | Minor impact on mission. | Minor negligible 1 |
| 2 | Deterioration of performance and mission. | Critical |
| Catastrophic | | 3 Safety hazard and/or likely loss of mission |
The "Criticality Ranking" is the product of the Failure Probability and the Mishap Severity. The criticality rating is a measure of how urgent and how severe mitigation actions will have to be taken, to reduce the Criticality Ranking.

Table 9: Criticality Ranking.
| Mishap Severity | Value | Effect of Failure Mode |
|-------------------|----------|--------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | Minor | This failure mode is not a concern. |
| 2 | Minor | This failure mode is of very minor concern. |
| 3 | Medium | Justification needed. Jury may decide to review. |
| 4 | High | Technical jury approval needed before launch. |
| 6 | Critical | Action required to reduce ranking before launch. |
| 9 | Critical | Action required to reduce ranking before launch. |
The output of the matrix is highlighting and ranking failure mode liabilities to the mission, and the justifications and mitigations to reduce the Criticality Ranking. An example is given below:

A typical FMECA scale for the complexity of projects attending LASC should feature no less than 10 identified, ranked, commented, and justified failure modes - these should address at the minimum all important and critical failure modes.

# Engineering Drawings and Optional Appendix
The final Mission Report appendix shall contain Engineering Drawings and optional appendices. This appendix shall include any revision controlled technical drawings necessary to define significant subsystems or components, and other optional appendices can include, but are not limited to further Subsystem Details, Detailed Structural and Mechanical Calculation, Detailed Logical Process Diagrams, Detailed Software Architecture, and Detailed Electrical Architecture.

# Technology and Operations Readiness Level
Each mission will be awarded as many as 550 points - 55% of 1,000 points possible - for their presentation and satellite launch operations at the Latin American Space Challenge, demonstrated by technology and operation readiness, successful communication and recovery.
The Technology Readiness constitutes 250 points of the total score assigned to the Technology and Operations Readiness Level. This includes completing an oral presentation, worth 100 points, and the successful demonstration of the minimum required objectives for a satellite during a workbench test, also worth 100 points (i.e., turning the satellite power on and demonstrating a real-time data collection via wireless or wired communication).
The Operation Readiness constitutes 50 points of the total score assigned to the Technology and Operations Readiness Level. This includes completing the Launch Readiness Review (LRR) with a green/approval flight status, worth 25 points, and the successful integration with an experimental rocket, also worth 25 points (i.e., completing the integration of the satellite with the experimental rocket).

The Successful Communication constitutes 150 points of the total score assigned to the Technology and Operations Readiness Level. This includes completing at least one receiving packet of data from the satellite during the launch operations, worth 50 points, and the successful data collecting from the satellite during the flight, also worth 50 points (i.e., collect data from the satellite during boost, coasting, apogee and descent phase). An additional 50 points will be assigned to satellite missions that demonstrate a wireless half or full-duplex communication.
The Successful Recovery of the satellite is worth 100 points of the overall value assigned to launch operations. A recovery operation is considered successful according to the following table of classification:
Table 18: Launch Operations Status and Recovery Points.

| Criterion | Outstanding | Recovery Points |
|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|
| No Launch | A Selected Mission either did not request a Launch Readiness Review (LRR), received a Launch Status equals to 'orange' or 'yellow' during the event, or received a "green" but did not attempt at least one ignition. | Zero |
| Launch Denied | A Selected Mission did not pass the Launch Readiness Review (LRR) due to one or multiple criteria being indicated as "red." | Zero |
| Operational or Launch Failure | The satellite suffered an indirect failure (e.g., Catastrophe At Take Off, No Ignition/Ignition Failure, etc.) or has not been found after an abnormal trajectory during the available recovery slots during the event. | Zero |
| Not Recovered | The satellite has not been found after the nominal trajectory during the available recovery slots during the event. | Zero |

# Dual-Challenge Bonus
A sounding rocket or a launch vehicle transports payloads for research and development, commercial purposes, education or training. An experimental rocket without a payload loses its purpose. The Latin American Space Challenge promotes the development of experimental satellites as part of the mission's payload.
Teams with Selected Missions in both the Rocket and Satellite Challenge may receive a bonus of 25 points if, and only if, the experimental satellite presented in the Satellite Challenge is placed in the experimental rocket and a launch is performed (i.e., the ignition of the propulsion system is initiated).
If two different teams coordinate to fly a satellite in the same experimental rocket, the Dual-Challenge Bonus may also be granted. However, both teams must inform event officials of this intent during the Launch Readiness Review (LRR)

# Final Score
The maximum points possible are up to 1,000 points, including the bonus points of participating with Selected Mission in both Rocket and Satellite Challenge.
Final Score = (Team Effort + Mission Report + Design Implementation + Technology and Operations Readiness Level) + Bonus Points - Penalties

# 1. Purpose
The Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) Lander Challenge Standards Manual (LCSM) is published to establish the standards, guidelines, and protocols necessary for participants to effectively engage in the LASC Lander Challenge.
This manual serves as the definitive reference for teams preparing and executing missions within the competition. It defines the criteria, requirements, and best practices to be followed throughout the lifecycle of the lander project, including design, development, and, where applicable, assembly, integration, testing, and low-altitude/low-velocity operations. It also provides the framework used to evaluate and assess the technical and operational aspects of all entries, ensuring alignment with best practices in space engineering and mission planning.

# 3. LASC Standards and Recommended Practices (LSARPs)
LSARPs in this manual are designed for use within the Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) and provide the criteria for evaluations. The LASC General Rules (LGR) document defines the Documentation System and LSARPs.

# 4. Manual Revisions
The document is subject to an annual revision, which invariably leads to a new edition. In the event that critical issues emerge impacting the content of the document, a revision to the current edition will be undertaken. All changes in this document are listed in the revision highlights table. For easier orientation, the following symbols identify any changes made within each section:
- Addition of a new item.
- Change to an item.
- Deletion of an item.

# 5. Acronyms and Definitions
AGL - Above Ground Level: The altitude of the rocket measured vertically from the launch pad ground elevation to the vehicle position or apogee.
All altitude requirements in the Rocket Challenge shall be measured in meters Above Ground Level (AGL) from the launch pad elevation. GPS receivers typically measure altitude relative to Mean Sea Level (MSL), therefore teams shall ensure proper conversion when reporting altitude data.
Caliber: A unit of length equal to the external diameter of the airframe body tube.
CG - Center of Gravity: The point at which the total mass of the vehicle is considered to be concentrated. The vehicle will balance at this point in any orientation.
CP - Center of Pressure: The point along the vehicle where the resultant aerodynamic forces are considered to act. The relative position between the Center of Pressure and the Center of Gravity determines the aerodynamic stability of the vehicle.
EGM - Event Guidance and Milestones: LASC Event Guidance and Milestones document available in the Documentation section of the LASC website.
LCC - Launch Control Center: The designated area from which launch operations are monitored and controlled, including vehicle status monitoring, safety coordination, and launch authorization.
LGR - LASC General Rules: LASC General Rules document available in the Documentation section of the LASC website.

LGR - LASC General Rules: LASC General Rules document available in the Documentation section of the LASC website.
LRR - Launch Readiness Review: A formal review conducted to verify that the vehicle, team, and operations are ready for launch.
LSARP - LASC Standards and Recommended Practices: Standards and recommended practices defined and detailed in the LASC General Rules (LGR) document.
<!-- image -->
MCC - Mission Control Center: The designated area from which mission operations are monitored and controlled after launch, including telemetry monitoring, flight tracking, command operations, and mission status management.
MSL - Mean Sea Level: Altitude measured relative to the average sea level, commonly used in aviation and GPS altitude measurements.
Payload: A single mass that does not interact with the overall operation of the vehicle. The mass must be a single piece (ie, not distributed in small pieces around the vehicle) and cannot be dual purpose with respect to vehicle operation.
Propulsion System: A single liquid or hybrid engine that provides the primary vertical lifting force of the vehicle.
No aerodynamic or air-breathing methods are permitted (ie, jets or fans). However, thrusters, solid motors and other in-space-type propulsion systems are permitted for control other than the primary lifting force (ie, attitude, roll, etc), except for in the TVC or Throttle Milestones (these other thrust sources are not considered part of the Propulsion System).

The intent is that all milestones use a full-scale propulsion system, as opposed to a subscale one. Event officials understand teams will iterate heavily, and possibly scrap, their engines as they progress - it is fine for the hop engine to perhaps be quite different from the engine used in the TVC milestone.
RAA - Rocket Assembly Area: Designated area where teams assemble, integrate, and prepare their rockets for flight.
RBF - Remove Before Flight: Safety tag or device that must be removed prior to launch or flight operations.
SF - Safety Factor: The ratio between the maximum load or stress a component can withstand and the maximum load or stress expected during operation. The safety factor is used to provide a margin against uncertainties, material variability, and unexpected loads.
Selected Mission: A mission proposal submitted by a team that has been evaluated, approved, and officially accepted by the LASC Organization for participation in the event.
SMS - Safety Management System: A systematic approach to managing safety, including organizational structure, responsibilities, policies, procedures, risk management, and continuous safety improvement processes.
R&D - Researched and Developed: A designation for hardware, software, or systems that have been primarily designed, developed, and built by the team, rather than purchased as complete commercial off-the-shelf solutions.
<!-- image -->

R&D - Researched and Developed: A designation for hardware, software, or systems that have been primarily designed, developed, and built by the team, rather than purchased as complete commercial off-the-shelf solutions.
<!-- image -->
Static Stability Margin: The distance between the Center of Gravity and the Center of Pressure divided by the airframe diameter, expressed in calibers.
Successful Landing: The vehicle must land gently VTVL style (ie, a vertical propulsive landing as opposed to a horizontal 'airplane' landing), and afterwards be in good condition, such that one may reasonably consider reusing the vehicle again with minor repair. Designed-to-fail landing systems, like crush structure legs, are not permitted. The entire vehicle that took off must land.
Tether: A physical restraint that prevents the vehicle from deviating substantially from a nominal flight area under the worst case scenario (i.e. throttle stuck open). The tether shall not assist the vehicle or substantially influence its flight during a nominal test (ie, while hovering).
<!-- image -->

# Section 1 - Lander Challenge (LND)
Section 1 defines the Lander Challenge (LND) mission categories, establishes the administrative requirements for application and registration, outlines all technical deliverables, and details the evaluation structure and awards.

# 1.1. Challenge Overview
The Lander Challenge is a long-term technical initiative structured around annual objectives for missions focused on the development of self-landing space vehicles. The initiative recognizes teams that achieve significant milestones toward the development of reusable launch systems or spacecraft capable of autonomous vertical landing.
The primary objective of the Lander Challenge is to enable and inspire teams to pursue the development of reusable, self-landing launch vehicles or planetary landers, while fostering expertise across the engineering and operational disciplines required for such systems.
Teams should note that the challenge does not necessarily require large-scale propulsion systems. For example, performing a 50-meter hop test does not inherently require a powerful engine or a full Thrust Vector Control (TVC) system. The challenge is designed to support incremental development approaches, serving as a stepping stone toward more advanced aerospace projects.

# Yearly Milestones
The challenge is structured around five major milestones, as illustrated in Figure 1, to be achieved within a period of up to five years, depending on the mission's development progress. Participation requires teams to actively develop a self-landing vehicle concept as the core element of their project.
Figure 1: Yearly Milestones.
<!-- image -->
Each year, the Latin American Space Challenge (LASC) opens an application process for new missions interested in joining the challenge. All applicable rules and requirements are defined within the LASC Documentation System and may be updated or amended by LASC at any time.
For the 2026 edition, the challenge will focus on the Mission Proposal and Concept Design phases of the Milestone 1. Participating teams are required to timely submit mission proposals,
<!-- image -->
development documentation, including progress updates, a mission patch, a pitch video, Milestone Report and presentation materials.
Starting in 2027, the organization intends to provide operational testing opportunities during the onsite event, enabling participating missions to test throttleable propulsion systems.

# 1.2. Mission Categories
Teams intending to participate in the LanderChallenge with a mission shall submit an Entry Application as defined in the LASC General Rules (LGR), indicating the desired mission category.
LND 1.2.1. Missions competing in the Lander Challenge shall develop a lander project under one of the following mission categories:
- Reusable Launch System (RLS) project; or
- Planetary Lander (Moon or Mars) project
The Lander Challenge is structured around five milestones for each mission category during a multi-year roadmap. These milestones follow a similar structure but include different technical requirements depending on the mission category.
The milestones serve as a development roadmap toward the primary objective of building advanced capabilities in complex aerospace systems for teams participating in the Latin American Space Challenge.

# Milestone 1 - Mission Proposal & Concept Design
Milestone 1 focuses on mission definition and system-level concept development. Participating teams with selected missions are expected to develop a mission proposal extending through 2030 and covering all planned milestones.
LND 1.3.1. Each mission shall define a mission concept for a self-landing vehicle corresponding to the selected mission category.
LND 1.3.2. Each mission shall conduct and present a Concept Design Review (CoDR) of the proposed system.
LND 1.3.3. The Concept Design Review (CoDR) shall present the overall system architecture, development approach, and programmatic planning for the mission.
LND 1.3.4. The Concept Design Review shall include, at a minimum, the following elements:
<!-- image -->
- a) System architecture and technical concept;
- b) Development budget estimation;
- c) Proposed project schedule and development timeline;
- d) Preliminary risk analysis.
LND 1.3.5. The Mission Proposal and Concept Design Review shall demonstrate the technical feasibility and development approach of the proposed mission.
LND 1.3.6. All documentation shall be submitted in accordance with the LASC Documentation System requirements established for the competition.
There are no category-specific technical performance requirements for Milestone 1 beyond the development of the Mission Proposal and Concept Design for the selected missions.

# General Requirements
LND 1.3.7. The Milestone 2 - Throttleable Hotfire requires each selected mission to perform, during the onsite event, a hot-fire test of a propulsion system with throttleable capabilities.
LND 1.3.8. The propulsion system shall demonstrate controlled thrust modulation during a continuous hot-fire test.
LND 1.3.9. Thrust modulation shall be achieved by controlling the propulsion system itself. The use of auxiliary systems such as gas thrusters to artificially modify thrust levels shall not be permitted for this milestone.
LND 1.3.10. Each mission shall submit a planned thrust trace prior to the hot-fire attempt.
LND 1.3.11. Each mission shall provide load cell data, control system data, and any other relevant telemetry demonstrating compliance with all requirements. Data shall be presented in a clear and organized format for evaluation by the judges.
LND 1.3.12. During throttle-down and throttle-up transitions, an additional tolerance window of 0.5 seconds shall be allowed for timing compliance

# Reusable Launch System (RLS) Requirements
LND 1.3.13. For Reusable Launch System (RLS) missions, the propulsion system shall operate for a minimum of 10 seconds of controlled thrust, excluding transient startup periods.
LND 1.3.14. The propulsion system shall demonstrate controlled and constant nominal thrust between 2 kN and 5 kN for a minimum duration of 5 seconds.
<!-- image -->
LND 1.3.15. The propulsion system shall smoothly throttle down to a thrust level within the range of 40% to 60% of the nominal thrust and shall maintain a constant thrust within this range for not less than 3 seconds.
LND 1.3.16. The propulsion system shall return to the same nominal thrust level and shall maintain constant thrust within the range of 2 kN to 5 kN for not less than 2 seconds.
LND 1.3.17. The planned thrust trace shall include an upper and lower tolerance band of ±10% of the planned nominal thrust.
LND 1.3.18. The measured thrust shall remain within the ±10% tolerance band centered on the planned nominal thrust throughout the thrust profile.
LND 1.3.19. During the nominal thrust hold period, the measured thrust shall remain above 1.8 kN for the entire duration.
LND 1.3.20. The low-thrust reference range (40% to 60%) shall be determined based on the lowest measured thrust value recorded during the nominal thrust hold period.

LND 1.3.19. During the nominal thrust hold period, the measured thrust shall remain above 1.8 kN for the entire duration.
LND 1.3.20. The low-thrust reference range (40% to 60%) shall be determined based on the lowest measured thrust value recorded during the nominal thrust hold period.
LND 1.3.21. During the low-thrust hold period, the measured thrust shall remain between 40% and 60% of the lowest thrust value measured during the nominal thrust hold period.

# Planetary Lander (Moon or Mars) Requirements
LND 1.3.22. For Reusable Launch System (RLS) missions, the propulsion system shall operate for a minimum of 20 seconds of controlled thrust, excluding transient startup periods.
LND 1.3.23. The propulsion system shall demonstrate controlled and constant nominal thrust between 0.8 kN and 2 kN for a minimum duration of 8 seconds.
LND 1.3.24. The propulsion system shall smoothly throttle down to a thrust level within the range of 35% to 45% of the nominal thrust and shall maintain a constant thrust within this range for not less than 4 seconds.
LND 1.3.25. The propulsion system shall return to the same nominal thrust level and shall maintain constant thrust within the range of 0.8 kN and 2 kN for not less than 8 seconds.
LND 1.3.26. The planned thrust trace shall include an upper and lower tolerance band of ±5% of the planned nominal thrust.
LND 1.3.27. The measured thrust shall remain within the ±5% tolerance band centered on the planned nominal thrust throughout the thrust profile.
LND 1.3.28. During the nominal thrust hold period, the measured thrust shall remain above 0.8 kN for the entire duration.
<!-- image -->
LND 1.3.29. The low-thrust reference range (35% to 45%) shall be determined based on the lowest measured thrust value recorded during the nominal thrust hold period.

<!-- image -->
LND 1.3.29. The low-thrust reference range (35% to 45%) shall be determined based on the lowest measured thrust value recorded during the nominal thrust hold period.
LND 1.3.30. During the low-thrust hold period, the measured thrust shall remain between 35% and 45% of the lowest thrust value measured during the nominal thrust hold period.

# General Requirements
LND 1.3.31. During the TVC hot-fire test, the propulsion system shall demonstrate thrust vector control by deflecting the thrust vector by a minimum angle of 7 (seven) degrees in all directions in a controlled manner, as determined acceptable by the judges.
LND 1.3.32. Thrust vector control shall be achieved by manipulation of the primary propulsion system. The use of external gas thrusters or reaction control thrusters to change the thrust vector during this milestone shall not be permitted.
LND 1.3.33. Alternative thrust vector control methods such as flow impingement, jet vanes, movable nozzles, or gimbaled engines shall be permitted.
LND 1.3.34. Vehicles may use gas thrusters or other attitude control systems during hover, drop, or hop tests in later milestones; however, such systems shall not be used to accomplish the TVC requirement of this milestone.
LND 1.3.35. Load cell data, control system data, actuator position data, and any other relevant telemetry demonstrating compliance with the thrust vector control requirements shall be provided in a clear and organized format for evaluation by the judges.

# Reusable Launch System (RLS) Requirements
LND 1.3.36. For Reusable Launch System (RLS) missions, the propulsion system shall operate within a thrust range of 2 kN to 5 kN for a minimum duration of 10 seconds during the TVC hot-fire test.

# Planetary Lander (Moon or Mars) Requirements
LND 1.3.37. For Planetary Lander missions, the propulsion system shall operate within a thrust range of 0.8 kN to 2 kN for a minimum duration of 20 seconds during the TVC hot-fire test.

# Milestone 4 - Tethered Hover
The Milestone 4 - Tethered Hover requires the vehicle to perform a controlled hover flight while attached to a tether system during the test.

# General Requirements
<!-- image -->
LND 1.3.38. The tether system shall be used as a safety restraint and shall not be used to actively control or stabilize the vehicle during the hover test.
LND 1.3.39. The vehicle shall demonstrate controlled hover flight using its propulsion and control systems.
LND 1.3.40. Each mission shall provide avionics data, acceleration data, altitude data, and any other relevant telemetry demonstrating compliance with all requirements. Data shall be presented in a clear and organized format for evaluation by the judges.
LND 1.3.41. For the purposes of this milestone, controlled hover shall be defined as maintaining vehicle position within a defined hover control volume during the hover period.
LND 1.3.42. Unless otherwise approved by the judges, the vehicle centroid shall remain within a 1-meter cubic volume during the hover test.
LND 1.3.43. The hover timer shall start when the vehicle first achieves stable hover and shall end when the vehicle leaves the hover control volume, lands, or thrust is reduced below hover thrust.

# Reusable Launch System (RLS) Requirements
LND 1.3.44. For Reusable Launch System (RLS) missions, the vehicle shall perform a controlled tethered hover for a minimum duration of 10 seconds while carrying at least 15 kg of payload mass.

# Planetary Lander (Moon or Mars) Requirements
LND 1.3.45. For Planetary Lander missions, the vehicle shall perform a controlled tethered hover for a minimum duration of 20 seconds while carrying at least 5 kg of payload mass.

# Milestone 5 - Hop Test Flight
The Milestone 5 - Hop Test Flight requires the vehicle to perform a controlled ascent, descent, and landing using the primary propulsion system while carrying the declared payload mass.

# General Requirements
LND 1.3.46. The vehicle shall take off from the ground, ascend to an altitude of 50 meters above ground level (AGL), and perform a controlled descent followed by a successful landing.
LND 1.3.47. The landing shall occur within a landing circle with a diameter of 10 meters located at a position selected by the judges.
LND 1.3.48. For a landing to be considered successful, the entire vehicle shall be contained within the vertical projection of the landing circle at the time of landing, as determined by the judges.
<!-- image -->
LND 1.3.49. Minor detachable or negligible materials such as thermal insulation fragments or small debris shall not be considered part of the vehicle for landing boundary determination, at the judges' discretion.
LND 1.3.50. The vehicle shall demonstrate controlled flight during ascent, hover (if performed), descent, and landing, as determined acceptable by the judges.
LND 1.3.51. Each mission shall provide avionics data, GNSS data, acceleration data, altitude data, and any other relevant telemetry demonstrating compliance with all requirements. Data shall be presented in a clear and organized format for evaluation.
LND 1.3.52. The vehicle shall include a flight termination system, kill switch, or equivalent safety system capable of shutting down the propulsion system in case of emergency.
LND 1.3.53. Teams shall follow safe operational practices and all safety requirements established for the event.

# Reusable Launch System (RLS) Requirements
LND 1.3.54. For Reusable Launch System (RLS) missions, the vehicle shall perform a controlled tethered hover for a minimum duration of 10 seconds while carrying a payload mass of not less than 15 kg.
LND 1.3.55. The vehicle shall take off and land within the same launch pad area.
LND 1.3.56. The total flight duration shall be not less than 10 seconds.
Planetary Lander (Moon or Mars) Requirements
LND 1.3.57. For Planetary Lander missions, the vehicle shall perform a controlled tethered hover for a minimum duration of 20 seconds while carrying a payload mass of not less than 5 kg.
LND 1.3.58. The vehicle shall take off from a designated launch pad and land at a designated landing zone located at a distance of not less than 150 meters from the launch pad.
LND 1.3.59. The total flight duration shall be not less than 20 seconds.

# Progress Updates
There will be four Progress Updates submitted per Selected Mission due to track mission progress in design and development. These forms will contain questions regarding safety, organization, launch operations and testing, propulsion, airframe and structures, avionics, recovery, and payload.
The template for each progress update will be available at least two weeks before the deadline to allow teams sufficient time to collect the appropriate information and data.
LND 1.4.1. Teams shall submit Progress Updates for each Selected Mission via the Latin American Space Challenge website (https://www.lasc.space/) on 4 (four) specific occasions prior to the onsite event: the Progress Update 1, Progress Update 2, Progress Update 3 and the Progress Update 4.
These Progress Updates will record progression in the project's technical characteristics during development. Event officials understand not all technical details will be known until later in the design process. Therefore, the Progress Updates prior to the final submission will be evaluated based only on their timeliness and completeness.
LND 1.4.2. Total completeness of the Progress Update form is required at all times. Reasonable engineering estimates and approximations are expected during the application process, but will be subject to progressive additional scrutiny in the subsequent Progress Updates.

Each mission should briefly mention their ongoing discussions and analysis in the comment fields for any numerical submissions that are known to be unreasonable or remain undecided.
LND 1.4.3. Teams should briefly mention their ongoing discussions and analysis in the comment fields for any numerical submissions that are known to be unreasonable or remain undecided.

# Marketing Actions
Since the early days of the Apollo missions, the general public has been captivated by the spectacle of rocket launches, eager for more information and the chance to witness these events firsthand. The allure of a launch vehicle is immense, drawing thousands of people to travel far from home to be near launch sites, just to hear the thunderous roar of rocket engines.
Event officials recognize the importance of structured marketing efforts to promote each team and its members. These efforts aim to increase visibility, attract attention to the missions, and help
<!-- image -->
support fundraising for each project. Such efforts are essential to realizing the dream of having thousands of spectators watch experimental rockets and satellites launch from LASC.
The Marketing Actions are divided in three deliverables: a Mission Patch, a Pitch Video and a competition for likes/followers in the following social media: Instagram, TikTok, X (Twitter), Facebook and LinkedIn.

# Mission Patch
Mission patches are emblems designed and worn by astronauts and people affiliated with a mission, such as Rocketeers and Satelliteers. The patches depict an image associated with the mission.
LND 1.4.4. Each team shall submit an appropriate Mission Patch for each Selected Mission during the Progress Update 1. Submissions shall be made in a timely manner using the designated form on the LASC Website, following the schedule outlined in the deliveries roadmap.
A Mission Patch must contain the mission and event references. The Mission Patch must include, at a minimum:
- The Mission ID and mission reference;
- The event reference;
- The milestone reference; and
- The year of the event.
For more information and examples, teams should access the following websites:

# 1.5. Scores, Evaluation and Awards
Event officials will evaluate competitors for the Milestone Award within each mission category based on the quality of required project documentation, the quality of their system's overall design and simulation, and the mission's overall excellence demonstrated at the Milestone Report.

# Scoring Categories and Grading Criteria
In 2026, lander missions will be scored in five different scoring categories or areas, which are (1) Team Effort, (2) the Milestone Report, and (3) the Presentation Session. These are weighted according to the following table. In each scoring category, a set of grading criteria is established. These criteria will be evaluated by the jury for each mission individually. Each grading criterion has several, more detailed, topics that establish what the jury will look for during the grading process.
Table 2: Weight of the Scoring Categories for the Rocket Challenge.
| Scoring Categories | Possible Points | %of Total Points |
|----------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 250 | 25% | Team Effort |
| 400 | 40% | Milestone Report |
| 350 | 35% | Presentation Session |
| TOTAL 1000 | 100% | |
These detailed topics are weighed equally within each criterion, while the main criteria are weighted differently within each mission category. The details of the grading criteria can be found in Appendix B: Detailed Grading Criteria.

# 1.6. Specific Awards
The lander Challenge awards the Milestone Winner in each category. Overall Awards are defined in the LASC General Rules (LGR) document.

# Best Experimental Lander of the Year
One team among the Milestone Winner Award in each category defined in this document will be named the Best Experimental Lander of the Year. The recipient of this award is determined by qualitative assessments of the event officials made throughout the entire event.
The Best Experimental Lander of the Year not necessarily will be the highest scoring mission, but will be awarded to a single team chosen by the event officials based on project complexity, technology and innovation.
A team is considered eligible for the award after participating in the Lander Challenge submitting all documents, reports, activities, and presenting the project.

# Milestone Winner Awards
A Milestone Winner Award will be granted to the highest scoring, eligible team in each of the Mission Categories defined in this document. A team is considered eligible for the place award(s) in its category after participating in the 2026 LASC submitting all documents, reports and activities.
In the event, if no teams meet this definition in a given category, event officials may issue Milestone Winner Awards at their discretion based on multiple factors - including points accrued, participation and engagement, and overall performance.

# Section 2 - Safety (SAF)
Section 3 details the Safety (SAF) policy and provisions for all missions, members, and teams to ensure event safety. This section covers the general guidelines for Satellite Missions.

# 2.1. Safety Policy
Safety is our Value number 1.
The Safety Management System (SMS) of the Latin American Space Challenge is composed of four pillars: Safety Policy, Safety Risk Management, Safety Assurance, and Safety Promotion.
All team members shall report any observed unsafe activities to event officials. The event will provide a specific channel for these reports, which will be anonymized before any potential action is taken by event officials.
The event will be guided by a Just Culture approach, where errors will be addressed through reeducation. However, any violation of rules will be treated as potential unruly behavior, with penalties or disqualification possible as outlined in Section 2.
During the Launch Readiness Review (LRR), event officials will use a checklist to approve an experimental rocket for loading with energetics and a rocket motor. Ensuring safety will be a key process for issuing any Flight Card during the event.
Event officials will conduct a safety risk management analysis before each launch to reduce the potential probabilities and/or severities of any operational risks.
Promotion is key to raising the bar among all participating teams. Event officials will conduct safety briefings to increase overall public awareness. Launching an experimental rocket is not trivial; the event has associated risks. All participants shall understand and accept these risks by signing a Waiver and Release of Liability to participate.

SAF 2.1.1. All participants shall follow the Latin American Space Challenge Safety Policy.
SAF 2.1.2. Participants in the Lander Challenge - Milestone 1 will not engage in hands-on activities, but must adhere to the Safety Policy at all times. The Mandatory Safety Guidance shall be followed during the experimental lander concept proposal.

# 2.2. Mandatory Safety Guidance
SAF 2.2.1. No energetics can be stored and/or transported in closed vessels or any other condition that may cause pressurization. Therefore, propellant grains shall be kept outside of casings and stored in proper containers.
<!-- image -->
SAF 2.2.2. All propulsion systems shall be inspected during the Hot-fire Test Readiness Review (HTRR) or Launch Readiness Review (LRR).
SAF 2.2.3. In case of hybrid propulsion systems, if the grain is made of a single segment, and is no longer than 600 mm, it may be inspected inside the combustion chamber. However, it is highly recommended that all propellant grains be outside for a complete inspection during the HTRR or LRR.
If the team (hybrids only), for any reason, chooses to place the grain inside the combustion chamber prior to inspection, it is a good practice to take pictures and videos of the grain and its installation. This may aid the officials to validate the structural integrity.
SAF 2.2.4. All heaters and igniters for propulsion systems shall be inspected during the HTRR or LRR before authorization for final assembly.
SAF 2.2.5. In case of liquid and/or hybrid propulsion systems, propellant tanks only will be loaded on the test bench or launch base with proper authorization from event officials and their supervision.

SAF 2.2.5. In case of liquid and/or hybrid propulsion systems, propellant tanks only will be loaded on the test bench or launch base with proper authorization from event officials and their supervision.
SAF 2.2.6. Teams with selected hybrid/liquid propulsion missions shall discuss their systems with their Rocketry Mentor, who will be responsible for contacting LASC to schedule a meeting. During this meeting, the team will present their system (including propellants, propellant masses, tanking configuration, feed system, and filling system) alongside their RM.
The RM shall schedule this meeting via email to lasc@lasc.space. During the meeting, event officials will define a safety distance for propellant filling and hose removal.
SAF 2.2.7. The personnel involved in launch operations and energetics integration shall wear Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). The minimum PPE required are the helmet and a signaling vest, temporarily provided by the event during the launch preparations. Additional PPE provided by the teams are welcome.
SAF 2.2.8. The personnel involved in launch operations and energetics integration shall wear full-length pants and closed shoes.

# Energetic Device Safing and Arming
An energetic device is considered armed when only one event is necessary to release the energy. For the purpose of this document, energetics are defined as all stored-energy devices - other than propulsion systems - that have reasonable potential to cause bodily injury upon energy release.
The following table lists some common types of stored-energy devices and overviews in what configuration they are considered non-energetic, safed, or armed.
<!-- image -->
Table 3: Energetic Devices and Configurations.
| Device Class | Non-energetic Safed | Armed |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Small igniters/squibs, nichrome, wire or similar | Large igniters with leads shunted | Igniters/Squibs Large igniters with shunted leads |
| Very small quantities contained in non-shrapnel producing devices (e.g., pyro-cutters or pyro-valves) | Large quantities with no igniter, shunted igniter leads, or igniter(s) connected to unpowered avionics | Pyrogens (e.g., black powder) Large quantities non-shunted igniter igniter(s) connected powered avionics |
| De-energized/relaxed state, small devices, or captured devices (i.e., no jettisoned parts)

| Pyrogens (e.g., black powder) Large quantities non-shunted igniter igniter(s) connected powered avionics |
| De-energized/relaxed state, small devices, or captured devices (i.e., no jettisoned parts) | Mechanically locked and not releasable by a single event | Mechanical Devices (e.g., powerful springs) Unlocked and releasable by a single event |
| Non-charged pressure vessels | Charged vessels with two events required to open main valve | Pressure Vessels Charged vessels one event required open main valve

|
Although these definitions are consistent with the propulsion system arming definition, this requirement is directed mainly at the energetics used by the termination system as well.
Note that propulsion systems may be armed only after the launch pad area is evacuated to a specified distance, this requirement permits personnel to arm other stored-energy devices at the launch rail.
SAF 2.3.1. All energetics shall be in the safe position/safed (i.e., 'remove before flight connected') until the experimental rocket is in the launch position, at which point they may be "armed". An energetic device is considered in the safe position/safed when two separate events are necessary to release the energy.
SAF 2.3.2. All energetic device arming features shall be externally accessible/controllable. This does not preclude the limited use of access panels which may be secured for flight while the vehicle is in the launch position.
SAF 2.3.3. All energetic device arming features shall be located on the airframe such that any inadvertent energy release by these devices will not impact personnel arming them.
For example, the arming key switch for an energetic device used to deploy a hatch panel shall not be located at the same airframe clocking position as the hatch panel deployed by that charge.

For example, the arming key switch for an energetic device used to deploy a hatch panel shall not be located at the same airframe clocking position as the hatch panel deployed by that charge.
SAF 2.3.4. The arming mechanism should be accessible from ground level, without the use of ladders or other elevation devices, when the rocket is at a vertical orientation on the launch pad. In addition, each implemented arming device shall be of easy access, taking a maximum of 3 (three) seconds to do it.
<!-- image -->

# 2.4. Pressure Vessels
The following requirements apply to the design and verification testing of all pressure vessels, including combustion chambers; however, combustion chambers are exempt from the relief device requirement.

# Burst Pressure for Metallic Pressure Vessels
SAF 2.4.1. Pressure vessels constructed entirely from isotropic materials (e.g., metals) shall be designed to a burst pressure no less than 2 times the maximum expected operating pressure, where the maximum operating pressure is the maximum pressure expected during pre-launch, flight, and recovery operations.

# Burst Pressure for Composite Pressure Vessels
SAF 2.4.2. Pressure vessels constructed entirely from non-isotropic materials (e.g., carbon fiber, fiberglass, fiber-reinforced plastics), as well as metallic pressure vessels with composite overwrap (Composite Overwrapped Pressure Vessels - COPVs), shall be designed to withstand a burst pressure of no less than three (3) times the Maximum Expected Operating Pressure (MEOP).
The Maximum Expected Operating Pressure (MEOP) is defined as the maximum pressure expected during pre-launch, flight, and recovery operations.

# Pressure Vessel Testing
The following requirements concern design and verification testing of pressure vessels.
SAF 2.4.3. Teams shall comply with all rules, regulations, and best practices imposed by the authorities at their chosen test location for pressure vessel testing.
SAF 2.4.4. Pressure vessels shall be proof pressure tested successfully to, at least, 1.5 times the maximum expected operating pressure for no less than twice the maximum expected system working time, using the intended or similar flight articles.
- The only acceptable type of test for this provision is the hydrostatic test.
- The maximum system working time is defined as the maximum uninterrupted time duration the vessel will remain pressurized during pre-launch, flight, and land operations.
- A full and detailed description of the used experimental methodology shall be included in the final report.
SAF 2.4.5. Teams should complete all tests at least three months prior to the test or launch window.
<!-- image -->

# Relief Device
SAF 2.4.6. Pressure vessels shall implement a relief device, set to open at no greater than the proof pressure specified in the following requirements. Combustion chambers are exempted from this requirement.
SAF 2.4.7. In hybrid/liquid propulsion systems, the venting or pressure relief shall be directed outwards the rocket. No venting is allowed into confined or semi-confined volumes.
<!-- image -->

# Section 3 - Propulsion System (PRS)
Section 5 defines the Propulsion System (PRS) for the experimental rocket projects, including types of propellants, ignition systems, and required tests.

# 3.1. Guidance
PRS 3.1.1. Teams shall utilize a liquid or hybrid researched and developed (R&D) propulsion system. An R&D propulsion system is defined as a propulsion system designed by the team, regardless of whether manufacturing is performed by the team or by a third party according to the team's design and specifications.
PRS 3.1.2. Multistage launch vehicles, side boosters, propulsion systems containing solid propellant, PET-bottles, water-based rockets, and toxic propellants are not allowed.
PRS 3.1.4. The minimum thrust-to-weight ratio for all mission categories shall be at least 1.25:1.
Thrust-to-weight ratio will be calculated based on either initial thrust of the motor or the average thrust of the motor (whichever is greater), divided by the takeoff weight (launch vehicle plus payload) of the experimental rocket.
PRS 3.1.5. Teams shall comply with all rules, regulations, and best practices imposed by the authorities at their chosen test location(s). The requirements concerning verification testing of researched and developed propulsion systems are detailed in the next provisions.
PRS 3.1.6. Teams should complete all tests 4 (four) months prior to the event. While not a requirement, this date is recommended to assure teams are prepared for the event.

# 3.2. Propellants
PRS 3.2.1. All propellants used shall be non-toxic. Nitrous oxide, kerosene, alcohol and similar substances, are all considered non-toxic.Toxic propellants are defined as those requiring breathing apparatus, special storage, transport infrastructure, extensive personal protective equipment (PPE), etc. (e.g., Hydrazine and N2O4).
PRS 3.2.2. Liquid oxygen (LOx), hydrogen peroxide and similar high oxidative fluids are prohibited and shall not be used due to its intrinsic risks, especially in an environment with high vegetation density.

# 3.3. Engineering Recommendations and Testing
PRS 3.3.1. Hybrid/liquid propulsion system shall implement a means for remotely controlled venting or offloading of all liquid and gaseous propellants in the event of a launch abort.
<!-- image -->
PRS 3.3.2. All hybrid/liquid combustion chambers shall be designed and tested according to the pressure vessel safety requirements defined in Section 2. Note that combustion chambers are exempted from the requirement for a relief device.
PRS 3.3.3. All propulsion systems using liquid or gaseous propellant(s) shall successfully (without significant anomalies) complete a propellant loading and off-loading test near as possible of the final "test or launch-configuration". Officials understand that tests may require additional or different setups for compliance to the location safety requirements.
Tests may be conducted using either actual propellant(s) or suitable proxy fluids. Links to videos and testing data shall be posted in your final report. A full and detailed description of the used experimental methodology shall be included in the final report.
PRS 3.3.4. While not a mandatory requirement, all propulsion systems should successfully (without significant anomalies) complete an instrumented (chamber pressure and/or thrust force), full scale (including system working time) static hot-fire test prior to the LASC.

PRS 3.3.4. While not a mandatory requirement, all propulsion systems should successfully (without significant anomalies) complete an instrumented (chamber pressure and/or thrust force), full scale (including system working time) static hot-fire test prior to the LASC.
This test does not need to be performed with the same tank parts, motor casing and/or nozzle components intended for use during the launch operations (e.g., teams shall verify their design but are not forced to design reloadable/reusable motor cases).

# Fill Monitoring
PRS 3.3.5. Each hybrid/liquid propulsion system shall have a fill monitoring system that alerts when the tank(s) has reached the desired fill state, whether that is a partial fill or a full fill.
It is recommended that a calibrated cantilever load cell is used to accurately measure and transmit to the launch controller the actual fill state. Other methods can be used, but assuming that the condensation plume can be seen during the Summer Season in Brazil may be not acceptable by event officials.
There are simple methods such as using a temperature sensor. A capacitive sensor is also another reliable solution. Filling times and fill status detection must be tested prior to arrival at the event..

# 3.4. Propulsion Safing and Arming
PRS 3.4.1. The 'arming action' is any action enabling an ignition signal to ignite the propellant(s).
PRS 3.4.2. A propulsion system is considered armed if only one action (e.g., an ignition signal) shall occur for the propellant(s) to ignite.
The "arming action" is usually something (i.e., a switch in series) that enables an ignition signal to ignite the propellant(s). For example, a software-based control circuit that automatically cycles through an "arm function" and an "ignition function" does not, in fact, implement arming.
<!-- image -->
In this case, the software's arm function does not prevent a single action (e.g., starting the launch software) from causing unauthorized ignition. This problem may be avoided by including a manual interrupt in the software program.
PRS 3.4.3. All propulsion system ignition circuits/sequences shall not be "armed" until all teams' launch personnel are at least 150 meters away from the launch vehicle.
PRS 3.4.4. The circuit arming shall be made by the event officials, after the Launch Area is clear, and only after all involved personnel are aware of this action.
PRS 3.4.5. Air-start ignition circuit arming is prohibited in any type of propulsion system designed for the mission categories of the Latin American Space Challenge.
<!-- image -->

# Section 4 - Airframe and Aerodynamics (STR)
Section 6 defines the Airframe and Aerodynamics (STR) overall guidance and requirements for the development of experimental lander projects, including material selection and prohibitions.
The following provisions address some key points applicable to the experimental lander projects, but are not exhaustive of the conditions affecting each unique design. Each team is responsible for thoroughly understanding, analyzing, and mitigating their design's unique load set.

# 4.1. Overall Structural Integrity
STR 4.1.1. Lander vehicles shall be constructed to withstand the operating stresses and retain structural integrity under the conditions encountered during handling transportation, and the hop operations.

# 4.2. Material Selection and Tests
STR 4.2.1. Experimental landers should be constructed from lightweight materials such as fiberglass, carbon fiber, and/or ductile lightweight metals like aluminum. The choice of materials and construction techniques should be appropriate for the specific requirements of the planned flight to ensure optimal performance and safety.
STR 4.2.3. The use of heavy metals should generally be minimized for your team's own benefits to overall vehicle mass fraction, cost, vehicle mass, and safety in recovery or motor failure events. The use of composite materials such as fiberglass, kevlar, carbon fiber, et al. as appropriate are strongly suggested. The use of natural composites such as wood, cardboard, hemp fiber composites, bamboo composites, etc. should be examined for effectiveness and appropriateness.
STR 4.2.5. Teams should simulate and/or test the properties of materials and the strength of components developed for use in their experimental rocket projects. This process should include both individual and joint analyses to ensure the integrity of all parts.
It is important to verify the suitability and durability of all materials and parts under the specific conditions they will face during their missions, as this is essential for ensuring launch safety.
STR 4.2.6. Environmentally hazardous materials shall not be used in the experimental rocket project. This includes lead, mercury, uranium, and any other radioactive materials.

# Load Bearing Eye Bolts and U-Bolts
STR 4.3.1. All load bearing eye bolts shall be of the closed-eye, forged type.
<!-- image -->
STR 4.3.2. All load bearing eye bolts, U-bolts, and links material shall be steel. This requirement extends to any bolt and eye-nut assembly used in place of an eyebolt. Stainless steel components (eye bolts, U-bolts, links, etc.) are permissible for use in recovery systems.

# Joining of Airframe Sections
STR 4.3.3. Airframe joints shall be constructed to prevent bending. This ensures structural integrity under operational stresses.
STR 4.3.4. Coupler tubes used to join two airframe sections that are not intended to separate during flight shall extend a minimum of one-half caliber into each adjoining airframe section. Extension of one full caliber into each section is recommended to improve structural integrity.

# Color Scheme and Markings
STR 4.3.5. All experimental anders shall be labeled with the Mission ID. The number assigned shall be clearly identified on the vehicle airframe, and prominently displayed, assisting officials to positively identify the project hardware with its respective team throughout the LASC. This label shall be duplicated on each part of the rocket which could separate either as designed or accidentally.
STR 4.3.6. Reference marks identifying the center of gravity and pressure shall be clearly placed on the airframe. These marks are essential for appropriate identification during the Launch Readiness Review (LRR), ensuring that all aspects of the lander's balance and aerodynamic stability are correctly assessed.
<!-- image -->

# Section 5 - Electronics and Control Systems (ECS)
Section 7 defines the Electronics and Control Systems (ECS) guidance and overall requirements, including electronics and control systems.

# 5.1. Official Altitude Logging System
ECS 5.1.1. All experimental landers shall incorporate a telemetry system capable of recording and transmitting flight data. The telemetry system may be either a Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) system or a team-developed researched and developed (R&D) system.
The following COTS telemetry systems are provided as recommended options:

| Featherweight | GPS Tracker with Ground Station (Full System) | Featherweight GPS Tracker |
