Officials will check and validate data transmitted and received from the telemetry system during the Launch Readiness Review (LRR).
ECS 5.1.2. Transmission/reception frequencies shall be coordinated before the opening of the Sugarcane Launch Range Launch Area. Teams may be told to change frequency to a different channel if there is overlap. Final frequencies will be shared for all teams' awareness and will be displayed on a board at the event.
ECS 5.1.3. Teams shall be able to disarm any type of flight computer and/or telemetry system from the outside of the experimental lander while it is still on the pad. Flight computers and/or telemetry systems shall be armed once the lander is on the pad and before igniter leads are connected in accordance with Safety Policies.

- → LiPo battery cell balancing circuits of up to 12S cell count are widely available as pre-assembled PCBs for a low price, complete with built-in under voltage-cut-off, overcurrent-protection and overcharging cut-off;
- → Vehicle batteries could all be considered 'permanently' installed, not requiring removal past initial installation during on-site preparation. The ground-based power supply should simply be outputting the battery trickle charge voltage, plus a diode drop, for easiest implementation.
The advantages of implementing such a system are in most cases worth the effort. Most significantly, the experimental vehicle is always tested with 100% peak charged batteries.
<!-- image -->

# Remove Before Flight (RBF)
ECS 5.2.2. The onboard batteries shall be connected to the electronics system via a "Normally Closed" switch or a Pull Pin Switch.
Given the risk of accidentally pulling the pin and thereby activating the electronics system, it is recommended to secure the end of the pin with a piece of masking tape. This precaution helps prevent unintentional removal and activation, ensuring the safety and stability of the system prior to intended use.
ECS 5.2.3. Teams shall attach Remove Before Flight (RBF) tags, flags, or protective covers to the following components:
- All pull-pin switches used in the experimental rocket
- All dynamic pressure ports or holes used for avionics
- The tip of the nosecone
In addition, teams should attach Remove Before Flight tags, flags, or protections to the following components
- Nozzle (Note: tags must not be fixed directly to the nozzle; they should be gently attached)
- All serviceable ports
- The leading edge of all fins
- Rail buttons
All Remove Before Flight (RBF) tags must be red with white lettering, displaying one of the following texts:
- 'RBF'
- 'Not for Flight'
- 'Remove Before Flight'
Each tag should also include an attached label or identification tag with specific component details, including component name, and item number.

# Safety Critical Wiring and Switch Requirements
Safety critical wiring is defined within this document as the electrical wiring that is involved with the deployment events of the recovery system. To facilitate the replacement of electronics in the event of a failure and to simplify assembly, teams should prioritize cable management.
Wiring and switches need to be securely fastened to prevent dislodging during launch and recovery operations. Given that experimental rockets will encounter high-G loads during boost phases and parachute deployment, which can displace wiring or toggle switches, it is crucial to secure these components firmly. Teams shall ensure that switches remain in the armed position
<!-- image -->
throughout the flight and do not accidentally deactivate during any flight events. This attention to detail in securing and managing wiring and switches is essential for maintaining the integrity and safety of the recovery system.
ECS 5.2.4. All safety critical wiring shall implement a cable management solution (e.g., wire ties, wiring, harnesses, cable raceways) which will prevent tangling and excessive free movement of significant wiring/cable lengths due to expected launch loads.
This requirement is not intended to negate the small amount of slack necessary at all connections/terminals to prevent unintentional de-mating due to expected launch loads transferred into wiring/cables at physical interfaces.

This requirement is not intended to negate the small amount of slack necessary at all connections/terminals to prevent unintentional de-mating due to expected launch loads transferred into wiring/cables at physical interfaces.
ECS 5.2.5. All safety critical wiring/cable connections shall be sufficiently secure as to prevent demating due to expected launch loads. This will be evaluated by a "tug test", in which the connection is gently but firmly "tugged" by hand to verify it is unlikely to break free in flight.

# 5.3. Attitude Control Systems (ACS)
Attitude Control Systems (ACS) requirements and procedures will be completely published at a later stage according to the official milestone roadmap and event schedule.

# Designed to Fail Safe
ECS 5.3.1. Attitude Control Systems (ACS) shall mechanically lock in a neutral state whenever either an abort signal is received for any reason, primary system power is lost, or the launch vehicle's attitude exceeds 15° from its launch elevation.
Any one of these conditions being met shall trigger the fail safe, neutral system state. A neutral state is defined as one which does not apply any moments to the launch vehicle (e.g., aerodynamic surfaces trimmed or retracted, gas jets off, etc.).

# Attitude Control System Electronics
ECS 5.3.2. Wherever possible, all ACS should comply with requirements and goals for "redundant electronics" and "safety critical wiring" as recovery systems. As for all electronics, it is highly recommended to ensure easy and quick access to switches/connectors via an access panel on the airframe. Access panels should be positioned so they are reachable from ground level, ideally without ladders. Access panels shall be secured for flight.

Section 6 shall define the Hot-Fire Test Operations (HFT) procedures and establish the minimum requirements for the throttleable hot-fire test (Milestone 2) and the Thrust Vector Control (TVC) test (Milestone 3) conducted as part of the Latin American Space Challenge milestones.
This section will be published at a later stage according to the official milestone roadmap and event schedule.

# Section 7 - Launch Operations (FLT)
Section 7 shall define the Launch Operations (FLT) procedures and specify the minimum technical, operational, and safety requirements applicable to the tethered flight test (Milestone 4) and the hop flight test (Milestone 5).
This section will be published at a later stage according to the official milestone roadmap and event schedule.

# Section 8 - General Requirements (GEN)
Section 7 defines the General Requirements (GEN) and establishes the overall guidance and requirements for the development of experimental lander projects. The following provisions address key requirements applicable to all lander projects, regardless of mission category, within the Latin American Space Challenge.

# 8.1. Administrative Requirements
GEN 8.1.1. Teams are expected to actively collaborate with other teams participating in the competition. This includes regularly sharing project updates, engaging with the technical community, sharing CAD models, test results, and technical lessons learned when appropriate, and, by default, inviting and attending each other's design reviews. Teams are encouraged to foster a collaborative environment and remain open to sharing resources and technical knowledge when possible.
GEN 8.1.2. Teams shall agree to host LASC Sponsors and Partners for meet-and-greet events and facility tours upon request from the LASC organization.
GEN 8.1.3. Each mission shall display LASC Sponsors and Partners logos on propulsion systems, experimental landers, and other major hardware in a prominent location, as requested by the LASC organization.

# Abstract
The Milestone Report shall include an Abstract. At a minimum, the abstract shall identify the mission category in which the team is competing, describe any unique or defining design characteristics of the project, and provide any additional information necessary to convey the high-level goals and objectives of the project.

# Introduction
The Milestone Report shall include an Introduction. This section shall provide an overview of the project, stakeholders, team structure, mission organization, and project management approach. The introduction may repeat some of the content included in the Abstract, as the Abstract is intended to function as a standalone summary when necessary.

# Mission Concept and Proposed Roadmap
The Milestone Report shall include a Mission Concept and Proposed Roadmap section. This section shall begin with the motivation for developing the experimental lander project, followed by the proposed mission concept, a detailed roadmap covering all remaining milestones, and an identification of the proposed propulsion system.

# System Architecture
The Milestone Report shall include a System Architecture Overview. This section shall present a top-level overview of the integrated system, including a cutaway or diagram depicting the fully integrated project and its major subsystems or modules configured for the proposed mission. This section shall also include a subsection specifically describing the proposed propulsion system.

# Concept of Operations
The Milestone Report shall include a Concept of Operations for each milestone described in the proposed roadmap. This section shall identify the mission phases, include mission profile figures, and describe the nominal operation of all subsystems or modules during each phase (i.e., what occurs during each phase and which subsystems are responsible for each function).

# Preliminary Budget
The Milestone Report shall include a Preliminary Budget for each milestone described in the proposed roadmap. This section shall identify the estimated capital investment and operational expenditures associated with each milestone in the proposed roadmap. A total program budget shall also be presented and discussed, including the expected sources of funding, sponsorships,

# Appendix A: Details for the Milestone Report
<!-- image -->
fundraising activities, and other resource acquisition strategies planned by the team to support the project throughout all milestones.

# Weights, Measures, and Performance Data
The Milestone Report shall contain preliminary Weights, Measures, and Performance Data of the experimental lander project. This requirement will be satisfied by informing the data in this section of the document.

# Lessons Learned and Next Steps
The Milestone Report shall include a Lessons Learned and Next Steps section. This section shall summarize lessons learned during concept development and design, including both team management and technical development aspects.
The section shall also define the next steps in the proposed roadmap. Additionally, this section should include strategies for knowledge transfer from senior team members to newer members who will assume future leadership and technical roles.

# Hazard Analysis Appendix
The first Milestone Report appendix shall contain a Hazard Analysis Report. This appendix shall address as applicable, hazardous material handling, transportation, storage procedures, and any other aspects of the design which pose potential hazards to operating personnel. A mitigation approach - by process and/or design - shall be defined for each hazard identified.

# Risk Assessment Appendix
The second Milestone Report appendix shall contain a Risk Assessment. This appendix shall summarize risk and reliability concepts associated with the project. All identified failure modes which pose a risk to mission success shall be recorded in a matrix, organized according to the mission phases identified by the ConOps.
A mitigation approach - by process and/or design - shall be defined for each risk identified. A common description of the Risk Assessment is FMECA (Failure Mode and Effect Criticality Analysis). A Risk Assessment/FMECA is often represented as a spreadsheet matrix. The input to the matrix is listed as follows:
- A description of the identified failure mode.
- The likelihood of the failure mode occurring.
- The severity and impact of the failure mode occurring
The likelihood of a failure mode occurrence and the severity of the occurrence is assigned values according to the following tables:
<!-- image -->
Table 8: Likelihood of Failure.
| Failure Probability Value | Assessment of Risk |
|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Remote 1 | This is unlikely to happen. |
| Occasional | 2 This might happen. |
| Probable or likely 3 | This is likely to happen. |
Table 9: Severity of Occurrence.
| Mishap Severity | Value | Effect of Failure Mode |
|---------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Minor or negligible | 1 Minor impact on mission. | |
| Critical | 2

| This is likely to happen. |
Table 9: Severity of Occurrence.
| Mishap Severity | Value | Effect of Failure Mode |
|---------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Minor or negligible | 1 Minor impact on mission. | |
| Critical | 2 | Deterioration of performance and mission. |
| Catastrophic | 3 | Safety hazard and/or likely loss of mission |
The "Criticality Ranking" is the product of the Failure Probability and the Mishap Severity. The criticality rating is a measure of how urgent and how severe mitigation actions will have to be taken, to reduce the Criticality Ranking.
Table 10: Criticality Ranking.
| Mishap Severity | Value | Effect of Failure Mode |
|-------------------|----------|--------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | Minor | This failure mode is not a concern. |
| 2 | Minor | This failure mode is of very minor concern. |
| 3 | Medium | Justification needed. Jury may decide to review. |
| 4 | High | Technical jury approval needed before launch. |
| 6 | Critical | Action required to reduce ranking before launch. |
| 9 | Critical | Action required to reduce ranking before launch. |
The output of the matrix is highlighting and ranking failure mode liabilities to the mission, and the justifications and mitigations to reduce the Criticality Ranking. An example is given below:
Table 11: Risk Matrix.
| Failure Mode | Mission Phase | Failure Probability | Mishap Severity | Critically Ranking

| Failure Mode | Mission Phase | Failure Probability | Mishap Severity | Critically Ranking | Mandatory Comments |
|-------------------------|------------------|-----------------------|-------------------|----------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Battery Thermal Runaway | Pre-launch phase | 1 | 3 | 3 | A thermal runaway begins when the heat generated within a battery exceeds the amount of heat that is dissipated to its surroundings. A battery balancing system will be implemented to reduce the risk. |
<!-- image -->
| Antenna Deployment failure | Deployment phase | 2 | 3 | 6 | Antenna deployment is mandatory for communication. A redundant system is required. |
|------------------------------|--------------------|-----|-----|-----|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
A typical FMECA scale for the complexity of projects attending LASC should feature no less than 10 identified, ranked, commented, and justified failure modes - these should address at the minimum all important and critical failure modes.

# Dynamics and kinematics
- Rigid, axisymmetric single-body rocket (no structural flex, no slosh, no aeroelasticity).
- Quasi-steady aerodynamics: (no unsteady/aero-memory effects).

# Environment and gravity
- Wind varies with altitude only.
- Flat-Earth/local-level frame; Earth rotation/Coriolis is considered.
- Gravity calculated with Somigliana (IGF80): gravity varies with latitude and altitude; Earth modelled as Ellipsoid

# Aerodynamics
- Static coefficients (Cd, CN, Cm, etc.) as functions of Mach and angle of attack; lookup/interpolation. User can define Custom
- No aerodynamic dynamic stall, (Unless user provides all aero coefficients)

# Recovery systems
- No cloth dynamics.
- Line stretch/reefing simplified; no canopy damage or oscillation aerodynamics beyond basic damping models.
- 3DoF parachute model

# SETTING UP AN ENVIRONMENT
First it's required to define where and when your rocket is going to be launched.
``` ：Environment( env 二 latitude=32.939377, longitude=-106.911986, elevation=1480 env.set_date(date=(2023, 6, 23, 17), timezone="America/Denver' ```
Then you need to choose and atmospheric model to simulate the launch conditions
``` _gfs.set_atmospheric_model(type="forecast file="GFS env env.max_expected_height 二 6000
```
The atmospheric model is what defines the wind data used in your simulation!
<!-- image -->

# ATMOSPHERIC MODELS
By default, when initializing an Environment class, the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) as defined by ISO 2533 is initialized. This means no wind and simplified pressure x height profiles.
But the user can also set a completely customized atmosphere.
```
env = Environment() # already /initializes the International Standard Atmosphere env_custom_atm.set_atmospheric_model( type="custom_atmosphere", pressure=None, temperature=300, wind_u=[ (0，5），#5m/sat0m (1000,10) #10 m/sat 1000 m wind_v=[ (0，-2),#-2m/sat0m (500,3)， #3m/sat500m (1600, 2), # 2 m/s at 1000 env_custom_atm.plots.atmospheric_model() ```
<!-- image -->

# FORECASTS AND WINDY ATMOSPHERE
Windy.com is a website that provides weather and atmospheric forecasts for any location worldwide. The same atmospheric predictions and data available on windy.com can be used in RocketPy.
The following models are accepted:
- ECMWF-HRES
- GFS
- ICON-Global
```
#we can use the ECMWF model from Windy.com. env windy.set_atmospheric_model(type="windy", file="EcMwF #TheGFS S model is also available on Windy.com. env_windy.set_atmospheric_model(type="windy", file="GFS #The IcoN model is a global weather forecasting model already a availableon Windy.com. env_windy.set_atmospheric_model(type="windy", file="Icon") ```
<!-- image -->

# REANALYSIS
Climate reanalyses combine past observations with models to generate consistent time series of multiple climate variables. One common reanalysis dataset is the ERA5, it needs to be downloaded and uploaded into the code.
This simulates past weather conditions and is useful for post-launch analysis
``` env_era5=Environment( date=(2018,10,15,12), latitude=39.389700, 1ongitude=-8.288964, elevation=113, filename e = "../data/weather/EuroC_pressure_levels_reanalysis_2001-2021.nc" env_era5.set_atmospheric_model( type="Reanalysis", file=filename, dictionary="ECMWF" ```
<!-- image -->

# ENSEMBLE
Ensemble forecasts are a set of forecasts generated by running a model multiple times with slightly different initial conditions.
``` 井 Ensemble env_gefs.set_atmospheric_model(type="Ensemble", file="GEFS' # Ensemble Reanalysis env_reanalysis.set_atmospheric_model( type="Ensemble' file="../data/weather/LASC2019_TATUI_reanalysis_ensemble.nc", dictionary="ECMwF' ```
<!-- image -->

# CREATING A MOTOR
RocketPy can simulate three different types of Motors. Each will require a different set of inputs
<!-- image -->
What we will need in general:
1. Motors thrust curve (Thrust x Time) 2. Propellant related information (Grains and Tanks/Fluids) 3. Motor physical parameters (Mass, Inertia, Position)
<!-- image -->

# Solid Motor
RocketPy does not simulate the motor performance, this is mainly defined by the thrust.
We will also need information regarding the propellant grains.
``` ProM2020 = SolidMotor( thrust_source="../../data/rockets/andromeda/thrust_curve.csv", burn_time=3.76, grain_number=1, grain_density=1468.9079097925262, grain_initial_inner_radius=0.01875, grain_outer_radius=0.0375, grain_initial_height=0.893, nozzle_radius=0.028125, nozzle_position=0, throat_radius=0.01875, grain_separation=0, grains_center_of_mass_position=0.5613903872259161, dry_inertia=(0, 0, 0), center_of_dry_mass_position=0.5, dry_mass=0, ```
<!-- image -->

# Solid Motor
RocketPy does not simulate the motor performance, this is mainly defined by the thrust.
We will also need information regarding the propellant grains.
``` ProM2020 = SolidMotor( thrust_source="../../data/rockets/andromeda/thrust_curve.csv" burn_time=3.76, grain_number=1, grain_density=1468.9079097925262, grain_initial_inner_radius=0.01875, grain_outer_radius=0.0375, grain_initial_height=0.893, nozzle_radius=0.028125, nozzle_position=0, throat_radius=0.01875, grain_separation=0, grains_center_of_mass_position=0.5613903872259161, dry_inertia=(0, 0, 0), center_of_dry_mass_position=0.5, dry_mass=0, ```

# TANKS
<!-- image -->
For Hybrids and Liquids we will need to define Tanks
There are 4 types of tanks:
MassFlowRateBasedTank
UllageBasedTank
MassBasedTank
LevelBasedTank
Tanks lets us calculate time varying properties, such as propellant mass and inertia variation. Functionally during the simulation they behave the same, the only difference is user input.
<!-- image -->

# 1. First we define the Fluids
``` liquid_N20 = Fluid(name="Liquid Nitrous 0xide", density=855) vapour_N20 = Fluid(name="vapour Nitrous 0xide", density=101) ```

# 2. Then we define the Tank Geometry (You can also define custom geometries)
cylindrical\_geometry = CylindricalTank(radius=0.1, height=2.0, spherical\_caps=False)
3. Define the tanks:
2. Each type of tank will require different type of time varying inputs 3. A Liquid/Hybrid motor can have multiple tanks
<!-- image -->

# CREATING A TANK
MassFlowRateBasedTank Example
``` N20_flow_tank = MassFlowRateBasedTank( name="MassFlowRateBasedTank", geometry=cylindrical_geometry, flux_time=24.750, liquid=liquid_N20, gas=vapour_N20, initial_liquid_mass=42.8, initial_gas_mass=0.1, liquid_mass_flow_rate_in=0, liquid_mass_flow_rate_out="../data/motors/liquid_motor_example/liquid_mass_flow_out.csv", gas_mass_flow_rate_in=0, gas_mass_flow_rate_out="../data/motors/liquid_motor_example/gas_mass_flow_out.csv", discretize=100, ```
- Pay special attention to the flux\_time parameter! Make sure it is coherent with motor burn\_time
- Pay special attention to the initial liquid/gas mass! It must be coherent with the flow rate curves
For the other tanks check out the docs: https://docs.rocketpy.org/en/latest/user/motors/tanks.html#
<!-- image -->

# AERODYNAMIC STABILITY
RocketPy also calculate the margin of static stability in calibers. The static stability basically only tells you if your fins are large enough to the vehicle has the tendency to zero the angle of attack.
<!-- image -->
| Conditions | First satage | Second stage |
|----------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Dead calm | 1.0 - 1.5 | 1.0 |
| Windy | 1.5 - 2.0 | 1.0 |
| Goes transonic | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| Very long, slender vehicle | add 1.0 | add 1.0 |
Suggested static margin in calibers.
Source: Newlands, R. (2016). Rocketry aerodynamics. AspireSpace Technical Papers.
<!-- image -->

# SETTING UP THE SIMULATION
With an environment defined and the rocket ready, the user only needs to define three more parameters to run a simulation:
- inclination: Rail inclination angle relative to ground, given in degrees;
- heading: Heading angle relative to north, given in degrees;
- rail\_length: Length in which the rocket will be attached to the rail.
```
#Juno III-Projeto Jupiter 2023 test_flight = Flight( rocket=juno, environment=env, inclination=85, heading=105, rtol=1e-6, atol=1e-6, max_time=600, rail_length=5.2, # Halcyon - Aerospace Team Graz - 2023 test_flight = Flight( rocket=HALcYoN, environment=env, inclination=85, heading=90, rail_length=12
```

- Gyroscopes
- Barometers
- GNSS (GPS)
- Perform Controlled Simulations
- Define Custom Aerodynamic Coefficients

# OPEN ROCKET TO ROCKETPY
We have a OpenRocket Converter: https://github.com/RocketPy-Team/RocketSerializer

# Installation
You can install I rocketserializer using pip:
pip install rocketserializer

# Requirements
Java
You need Java to be installed on your system to use rocketserializer . We recommend downloading Java 17, which is required to run OpenRocket-23.09.
https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/downloads/

# OpenRocket
You also need to download the OpenRocket JAR file. You can download it from the following link:
https://openrocket.info/downloads.html?vers= 23.09#content-JAR
<!-- image -->

# Creating a simulation notebook
ork2notebook --filepath your\_rocket.ork

# Limitations
This code won't work for your rocket if it has any of the following features:
- Your .ork file must be saved in English
- Your .ork file must be saved with at least 1 simulation data
- Only one single stage is supported
- Only a single motor is supported
- Only a single nose cone is supported

# OPEN ROCKET TO ROCKETPY
The options are the following:
- --filepath : The .ork file to be serialized.
- --output : Path to the output folder. If not set, the output will be saved in the same folder as the filepath
- --ork\_jar : Specify the path to the OpenRocket jar file. If not set, the library will try to find the jar file in the current directory.
- --encoding : The encoding of the .ork file. By default, it is set to utf-8
- --verbose : If you want to see the progress of the serialization, set this option to True. By default, it is set to False.
